items.title: Contributing to streamplace
items.author: ElshadHu
items.content:
Nowadays I've been reading and writing Go code regularly, and my Go journey started with A Tour of Go. While building my http-server-ts, I watched this video where Primeagen builds an HTTP server in Go, which showed me how Go handles network programming. After getting confident with the language specifics, I found streamplace. I tackled that issue and successfully made a PR that got merged. I'll give you the details below.

When the firehose is unavailable, the stream had error-prone code that would crash the loop. After getting familiar with the codebase (without even setting it up, thanks to my C++ background), I commented on the issue saying I'd like to work on it. I mentioned that the issue was in the function in , and I was planning to replace the crash with a backoff mechanism. I got nice feedback from the maintainer saying my approach sounded reasonable and that it would be better to add a metric to . Basically, my conversation with the maintainer reduced the back and forth in my pull request, which I recommend to everybody that communicate beforehand when tackling an issue.

Even though the code structure was pretty niche, setting up this project was time-consuming because of handling dependencies. I spent way too much time on this, which I should have done a long time ago if I wanted to contribute regularly. I dealt with version issues when following the documentation.

I fixed the issue by adding a streamplace_labeler_firehoses_connected metric in to track labeler connections:

Then I imported and changed the log level at the retry threshold from to :

When fixing this bug, I made a mistake that I corrected before even committing. I was unintentionally creating a race condition by changing :

This was problematic because the won't execute until the function returns, but I also had which would be called in the reverse order of declaration. This was creating confusion. When it comes to critical-performance places in the code, it's always better to be skeptical. For that reason, I didn't touch and it stayed as .

Testing and Suggestion from Maintainer

I tested the change locally using Bluesky's official moderation labeler. I made sure that the metric reports 1 while the labeler firehose is connected. I also tested the retry path and saw that the code backs off for 5 seconds after repeated failures instead of crashing.

But I didn't consider more than one labeler firehose by writing:

Via the suggestion from the maintainer, I changed to and instead of for connecting more than one labeler firehose:

Eventually my pull request got merged.

For the first time, I dealt with a well-structured Go codebase. I learned about race conditions and reminded myself how important small details are. Also, as I mentioned before, it's better to keep code changes precise and stay in touch with maintainers. I'll keep going and continue contributing to this codebase.

items.title: Go Slices: The Pointer Paradox Why Your Appends Disappear (Understanding when slice modifications persist and when they vanish)
items.author: Moksh
items.content:
Ever faced a situation where modifying a slice inside a helper function updates the original data , but appending mysteriously does nothing?

Welcome to the .

Why does but Charlie disappears into the void?

LetвҖҷs unpack the internals.

Understanding WhatвҖҷs Inside a Slice

A Go slice is .

It is a tiny :

  • Pointer to underlying array

When passed to a function, , not the underlying data.

The Pointer Double Whammy in

With , there are :

  1. Slice header вҶ’ points to array
  2. Array elements вҶ’ pointers to user structs 
users (slice header)
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ [pointer1, pointer2] (array of pointers)
    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ          в””в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ Bob struct
    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ
    в”Ӯ        в””в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ Alice struct
    в”Ӯ
    в””в”Җв”Җ length: 2, capacity: 2

Rule 1: Struct modifications persist

Because both caller and callee share the same pointer to Alice.

Rule 2: Slice appends vanish

may allocate a and produces a , but only .

The caller still points to the .

main.users вҶ’ [ptr1, ptr2] (len=2, cap=2)

Inside function after append

updateUsers.users вҶ’ [ptr1, ptr2, ptr3] (len=3, cap=4)
main.users        вҶ’ unchanged

Charlie вҶ’ eligible for GC

Fixing Appends (Make Them Persist)

Option 1: Pass a pointer to the slice

Option 2: Return the updated slice

Option 3: Use methods on custom types

  • When you update the fields of a struct through a pointer inside a slice, the changes are reflected in the original data because both the caller and the callee reference the same memory.
  • When you modify the (length or capacity), those changes do carry over because the slice header is passed .

  • As a result, using inside a function wonвҖҷt affect the callerвҖҷs slice unless:

    • you return the modified slice, or
    • you pass a pointer to the slice.

Pointers in slices share memory, but slice headers donвҖҷt. If you grow a slice in another function, pass a pointer or return it.

If you found this useful, share it with a Go dev whoвҖҷs currently yelling at their debugger рҹҳү

items.title: Love for Go and Rust rant!
items.author: Bart van Deenen
items.content:
I'm so tired of this shit. Every few weeks, some Rustacean/Rustafarian/Rustbucket slithers in here with their smug "but have you tried fearless concurrency?" spiel, acting like Rust is the Second Coming of programming languages that finally fixes everything wrong with the world. Newsflash: it's not. It's a bloated, over-engineered mess masquerading as a panacea, and its concurrency story is one of the biggest hyped-up lies in modern PL discourse.

Go (and yeah, Erlang/BEAM before it) actually got concurrency right. Goroutines are dirt-cheap, channels are built-in and idiomatic, the scheduler is battle-tested, and you can spin up thousandsвҖ”hell, millionsвҖ”of concurrent tasks without your brain melting or the compiler throwing a tantrum. It's simple, predictable, and scales to real-world production systems without needing a PhD in lifetime annotations.

Rust? Oh boy. They brag about "fearless concurrency" because the borrow checker catches data races at compile time. Cool story, bro. But guess what? That's only half the battleвҖ”and a tiny half at that. Real concurrency bugs aren't just shared mutable state; they're deadlocks, livelocks, starvation, priority inversion, and worst of all: blocking the damn executor.

In Rust's dominant async ecosystem (Tokio, async-std, whatever flavor of the week), everything's cooperative multitasking on a thread pool. One idiot calls a blocking functionвҖ”.await a future that secretly does std:рҹ§ө:sleep, or reads a file synchronously, or hits some crate that blocks under the hoodвҖ”and BAM, you've starved the entire runtime. Your "massively concurrent" app grinds to a halt on one thread while the rest sit idle. Sound familiar? It's the exact same footgun as Python's GIL or Java's thread-blocking nonsense. Rust doesn't prevent it; it just makes you chase it down manually, wrap it in spawn_blocking, or pray your dependencies are perfectly async-all-the-way-down (spoiler: they're not).

And don't give me that "but Send/Sync traits!" crap. Traits don't magically make third-party libraries non-blocking. One lazy dev (or one outdated crate) and your fearless concurrency is as vulnerable as any GC'd language. Meanwhile in Go, you just go func() and communicate over channelsвҖ”it's hard to accidentally block the world because the model is designed for real engineers building real systems, not theoretical purity.

Rustaceans act like their language is the cure-all because the compiler yells at you for borrowing wrong. Big whoopвҖ”that's like bragging your car has the best seatbelts while ignoring it has no engine. The borrow checker is great for memory safety (congrats, you reinvented GC without the GC), but it comes at the cost of endless fighting the compiler, lifetimes in every signature, and async code that's "colored" and infectious, turning your whole codebase into a pin-projecting nightmare.

Go gets shit done. Fast compile times, single binary deploys, boring readability that onboard new devs in days, and concurrency that's actually enjoyable. Rust? Steep learning cliff, borrow errors that make you question your life choices, and a community of evangelists who think verbosity = virtue.

Rust is fine for what it is: low-level systems crap where you need zero-cost abstractions and no GC pauses. But for servers, backends, networked servicesвҖ”the stuff we do in r/golang? It's overkill wrapped in hype. Stop shilling your cult language here. We have goroutines. We have channels. We have simplicity that scales.

Go is king. Rust is just the loud kid in the back yelling "but my ownership model!"

Downvote me, Rustbucks. I'll be over here shipping code while you're still wrestling the compiler.

items.title: 1M+ Req/s Heavy-Read API in Go. Production Lessons Learned
items.author: KhГәc Ngб»Қc Huy
items.content:
Today, I'm sharing a Distributed In-Memory Cache architecture specifically designed for , complete with a demo and benchmark.

The "pattern" described below is synthesized from a production architecture implemented at my company. It has been modified and "AI-refined" for public sharing.

The Challenge: Scaling Read-Heavy Applications

Imagine hitting a database or even a Redis instance with 1 million requests per second (10^6 Req/s). That's not sustainable without significant investment. If we do the math, using traditional vertical scaling (beefing up the DB/Redis servers) becomes incredibly expensive, fast.

Consider an :

  • A handles product creation/updates (sellers posting products).
  • A handles product viewing (buyers browsing products).

Since the ratio of product views to product posts is usually massive, it's prudent to separate these functionalities into at least two distinct microservices: a and a . This separation ensures that if the Write Service experiences an outage, the Read Service (the critical path for user experience) remains unaffected.

So, how do we handle the intense read load?

The Proposed Architecture: Event-Driven Cache Synchronization

Here is the high-level approach to decouple the Read Service from the main data store:

When the seller successfully creates or updates a product ():

  1. The Write Service performs the database operation.
  2. It then (the updated data) to a message broker (e.g., Redis Pub/Sub).
  3. All instances (pods) of the subscribe and listen for this event.
  4. Upon receiving the event, each Read Service pod updates its own .

At first glance, this might seem standard, but the real power is in the that significantly reduce overhead.

The "Secret Sauce": Eliminating CPU and I/O Bottlenecks

After intense monitoring (metrics, APM, etc.), we noticed unusual spikes in service overhead during peak load. Reviewing the code and benchmarking led us to three crucial optimizations:

1. Zero-Copy Cache Update

When the Write Service publishes an update event to Redis, it first serializes (marshals) the object into raw bytes.

Object -> Serialize -> Bytes -> Publish to Redis

Crucially, when the Read Service pods listen for this event, they do not deserialize (unmarshal) the object back. They simply take the raw bytes and store them directly into the local In-Memory Cache.

2. Bypassing the Standard Framework Overhead

We initially used a framework (like Go Fiber) for our Read Service. However, we switched to using Go's .

The key optimization: the Read Service directly serves the retrieved from the In-Memory Cache as the HTTP response body.

Local Cache (Bytes) -> net/http -> HTTP Response (Bytes)

By combining steps 1 and 2, we have essentially eliminated all CPU-bound serialization/deserialization and I/O-bound database/remote cache lookups from the critical read path.

3. Leveraging HTTP 304 Caching (Etag)

To minimize network overhead, we implemented HTTP 304 caching.

  • The Write Service calculates the (a hash of the object) when it publishes the event.
  • This ETag is passed along to the Read Service pods.
  • The Read Service uses this ETag in its responses. If the client sends an header with the matching ETag, the Read Service returns a , saving bandwidth and processing time.

4. Response Compression (Gzip/Brotli)

While optional (and we temporarily skipped it due to client-side complexity), the ideal is to also handle Gzip/Brotli compression at the . This means the compressed bytes are stored in the cache, further reducing network overhead without impacting the Read Service's CPU budget.

Architectural Implications

Here are the key takeaways from adopting this pattern:

  • Database and Remote Cache are removed from the Critical Path: The DB and Redis are only used for event pub/sub and as a fallback if a new pod needs to bootstrap its cache. This allows for significantly lower capacity requirements for these vertical components.
  • Massive Horizontal Scaling: Scaling Read Service pods (horizontal scaling) is orders of magnitude cheaper and easier than scaling the DB/Redis (vertical scaling). In our production environment, each pod can handle 60,000 Req/s, meaning <20 pods are needed for the 1 million Req/s target.
  • This architecture inherently follows the Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) principle by distinctly separating the data update (Command/Write) path from the data retrieval (Query/Read) path.
  • By isolating and optimizing a single, high-traffic read operation to this degree, it approaches the design philosophy of a "nano-service," similar to an AWS Lambda function handling a single task.

The Core Architecture Flow

Sub-millisecond P99 response times on cache hits.
Adding more Reader pods scales throughput proportionally.
Auto-propagation of updates via Pub/Sub with minimal overhead.
Minimal serialization/deserialization And Smart caching (LFU/LRU).

The demo includes three comparative endpoints. Results on my test machine (4 threads / 400 concurrent users):

Local cache + raw bytes (no marshal)
Reads JSON directly from Redis
Local cache + on read

The difference between the raw bytes approach () and the one that requires JSON marshalling () clearly shows the value of eliminating CPU-bound serialization.

Real Metrics: Production Scale and Efficiency

Beyond the low-latency benchmarks, the true value of this architecture is seen in its and

Production Resource Utilization

Aggregate throughput for the Heavy-Read API.
Throughput achieved by a single, standard Kubernetes pod.
The maximum number of pods needed to handle the peak 1M Req/s load.
Used only for Pub/Sub events and occasional cache miss bootstrap.

The heavy-read example focuses on speed and architecture. In real systems you also need to guard against stale data (out-of-order messages, missed invalidations, network partitions).

For a full, code-level treatment of this problem (versioned entries, detector, and -based validation), see:

That example shows how to reject stale updates, detect stale entries on , and handle Redis failures.

The Distributed In-Memory Cache pattern is more than just a performance tweak; it is a fundamental shift in how we handle read traffic in event-driven microservices.

This approach prioritizes and over strict (it adheres to the side of the ). There will be a brief period of eventual consistency while the event propagates. When adopting this, ensure your business logic can tolerate minor, temporary data inconsistencies.

Give the gitbub repository a star and a watch! Your support encourages me to write the next deep dive on

items.title: Kafka Consumer Health Checks: Dead or Alive
items.author: Myroslav Vivcharyk
items.content:
Many of you have been there - itвҖҷs 3 AM, your phone buzzes, and youвҖҷre staring at an alert: вҖңKafka consumer lag exceeded threshold.вҖқ

You stumble to your laptop, check the metrics, andвҖҰ everything looks fine. The messages are processing. The lag was just a temporary spike from a slow downstream service. You silence the alert and go back to bed, mentally adding another item to your "fix someday" list.

Here's what's actually happening: lag tells you how far behind you are, but not whether you're making progress. A consumer can sit at 1000 messages lag for 10 minutes because it's stuck, or because it's processing at exactly the rate messages arrive. From lag alone, you can't tell the difference.

The real question isn't "how much lag?" вҖ” it's "are we making progress?"

ThatвҖҷs the problem I found myself wrestling with a while back. After dealing with false positive alerts and delayed detection of real issues, I discovered a simple but brilliant solution from PagerDutyвҖҷs engineering team. Rather than retelling their story, I want to show you how to implement this approach in Go, with some insights IвҖҷve picked up along the way.

Why Traditional Health Checks Fall Short

LetвҖҷs talk about common patterns and why they donвҖҷt quite work:

The вҖңAlways HealthyвҖқ Approach

This tells you nothing. Your consumer could be completely frozen, and Kubernetes (any orchestrator) would happily keep it alive.

The вҖңPing the BrokerвҖқ Approach

This is better than nothing вҖ” at least youвҖҷre verifying connectivity. But connectivity doesnвҖҷt mean your consumer is processing messages. Your network might be fine while your consumer group is stuck in an infinite rebalance loop.

This is where most teams land. YouвҖҷre probably already tracking consumer lag through Prometheus or similar tools. YouвҖҷve set up alerts when lag exceeds some threshold вҖ” maybe 100 messages, maybe 1000, maybe a million.

But hereвҖҷs the problem: what should that threshold be?

вҶ’ You wake up at 3 AM because a downstream API responded slowly for 30 seconds. The consumer is fine, just briefly overwhelmed. False alarm.

вҶ’ You miss genuine issues until theyвҖҷve already cascaded into customer-visible problems. By the time your alert fires, youвҖҷre in damage control mode.

: A single stuck POD in your deployment of 50 can go unnoticed if youвҖҷre monitoring average lag. That POD quietly fails while the other 49 keep working, masking the issue in your aggregate metrics.

The fundamental tension: fast detection means false positives, reliable alerts mean delayed detection.

You can build sophisticated heuristics or even ML models to detect anomalies, but all of that adds complexity and still detects problems with noticeable delay.

The Key Insight: Progress vs. Position

What we really need is a way to answer a simple question: Is this specific consumer instance making progress?

Instead of measuring lag, we measure . Here's the approach:

: Track the timestamp of the last processed message for each partition. If we're processing messages, we're healthy.

: If enough time passes without new messages (say, X seconds), we don't panic yet. We query the Kafka broker for the latest offset. Now we can make a decision:

  • Consumer Offset < Broker Offset: вқҢ (there are messages available, but we're not processing them вҖ” we're stuck)
  • Consumer Offset вүҘ Broker Offset: вң… (we're caught up, just waiting for more work вҖ” we're idle)

This elegantly distinguishes between a stuck consumer and a consumer that's simply idle.

How It Works: Three Scenarios

Let me show you exactly what happens in each case.

Scenario 1: Active Processing (Healthy)

When messages are flowing and your consumer is processing them, health checks are instant:

No broker queries needed вҖ” we've seen recent activity.

Scenario 2: Stuck Consumer (Unhealthy)

The consumer freezes, but messages keep arriving:

The broker query reveals there's work to do, but we're not doing it.

Scenario 3: Idle Consumer (Healthy)

The consumer is caught up, just waiting for new messages:

This is the key distinction вҖ” same timeout, different outcome based on broker state.

The beauty of this approach is its simplicity вҖ” we're not doing complex analysis or ML. We're just asking two questions: "Have I seen new work? If not, is there new work available?"

Let me walk you through how to use it.

The setup is straightforward. Here's what you need with Sarama:

? This defines how long we'll wait without seeing new messages before we query the broker. Set this based on your expected message frequency:

  • High-throughput topics: 10-30 seconds works well
  • Medium-volume topics: 1-2 minutes is reasonable
  • Low-volume topics: You might need 5-10 minutes

The key is balancing detection speed with broker query overhead. Too short and youвҖҷll query the broker constantly during idle periods. Too long and youвҖҷll delay detecting real issues.

Integrating with Your Consumer

The integration is minimal вҖ” just call for each message you process:

ThatвҖҷs it. The monitor now knows when youвҖҷve processed a message and can track progress per partition.

Exposing the Health Check

Wire this into your HTTP server for health checks:

Now your orchestrator (Kubernetes, ECS, whatever) can hit this endpoint and know the real health of your consumer.

The Critical Detail: Handling Rebalances

HereвҖҷs where custom implementations may fail. In Kafka, partition ownership is fluid. If you add a new POD or an old one crashes, Kafka triggers a rebalance. Your consumer instance might lose ownership of and gain an ownership of .

The Danger of the "Zombie Partition"

Without proper cleanup, here's what happens:

  1. : You added a new POD to consumer group, or an existing one crashes. Kafka initiates a consumer group rebalance to redistribute partition ownership.
  2. Your consumer loses ownership of and stops reading from it (correctly - you no longer own it)
  3. The health checker still holds a reference to in its memory
  4. Time passes without new messages for in your tracker (obviously вҖ” you're not reading it anymore)
  5. The health checker eventually queries the broker and sees the offset for is moving (thanks to whoever owns it now)
  6. But your local tracked offset for is frozen at the last message you processed before losing ownership
  7. : The health checker flags your pod as for a partition it doesn't even own anymore

This is a "zombie partition" problem вҖ” your health checker is tracking ghosts.

You must manage the partition lifecycle. Call when a session ends to purge that partition from the monitorвҖҷs memory:

The signal from Sarama indicates that the session is ending вҖ” either due to a rebalance, shutdown, or error. This is your cue to clean up tracking state for this partition.

: In a production system with frequent deployments, rebalances happen constantly. Without proper cleanup, youвҖҷll see cascading false alerts as PODs report unhealthy for partitions theyвҖҷve lost ownership of.

The libraryвҖҷs design is intentionally simple. Here are the key pieces:

We maintain a map of topic-partitions to their last-seen offset and timestamp:

Because each partition is independent. being stuck doesnвҖҷt mean is stuck. We need per-partition granularity to avoid false positives.

The verification follows the two-step process:

The key insight is in that comparison: offsetTimestamp.Offset < latestOffset.

What About Poison Pill Messages?

A common pitfall in health checks is confusing activity with progress. If a consumer is stuck in an infinite retry loop on a single malformed message, itвҖҷs technically вҖңactiveвҖқ вҖ” burning CPU and making network calls вҖ” but itвҖҷs not вҖңhealthyвҖқ because the stream is blocked.

This approach handles it correctly. HereвҖҷs why:

The tracker only updates the timestamp when the offset actually moves forward:

If your consumer spends 10 minutes retrying the same message at offset 100, the offset never advances. The timestamp stays frozen at the last successful message. Eventually the timeout expires, and because the broker has newer messages (101, 102, 103вҖҰ), the health check correctly reports .

This forces visibility on вҖңzombie loopsвҖқ that may otherwise go unnoticed.

Broker Outages: Choosing Your Failure Mode

Since our verification step requires querying the broker, you need to decide what happens when that call fails.

The library exposes an flag that controls this behavior:

  • : You never report вҖңHealthyвҖқ when youвҖҷre actually blind to the real state
  • : A temporary Kafka blip can cause Kubernetes to restart your entire consumer fleet simultaneously (a вҖңrestart stormвҖқ). Since restarting wonвҖҷt fix the broker, this just adds chaos to an ongoing outage
  • : Your PODs stay running, warm, and ready to resume work the moment the broker recovers
  • : You might miss a legitimate network partition affecting only your specific POD

: Start with fail-closed (the default). It's safer вҖ” you never report healthy when you're blind to the actual state. If you experience restart storms during broker incidents, switch to fail-open and add separate monitoring for broker connectivity at the infrastructure level.

Does This Replace Consumer Lag Monitoring?

No! Consumer lag metrics are still valuable for:

  • : Understanding if you need to scale your consumer group
  • : Tracking how your processing speed changes over time
  • : If your requirement is вҖңprocess messages within 5 minutes,вҖқ lag is exactly what you need to measure

This health check complements lag monitoring вҖ” it doesn't replace it. Lag tells you if you need more consumers. Health checks tell you if your existing consumers are working.

The core insight is simple: health checks should measure progress.

By distinguishing between вҖңstuck with work to doвҖқ and вҖңidle but caught up,вҖқ we eliminate false positives without missing real issues. No more 3 AM alerts for temporary slowdowns. No more delayed detection of genuinely stuck consumers.

The implementation is straightforward:

  1. Track offset progression for each partition
  2. When idle too long, verify against broker state
  3. Handle rebalances cleanly with proper partition lifecycle management
  4. Choose your failure mode for broker errors

Whether you use or implement this pattern yourself, itвҖҷs universal across languages and Kafka clients. The principle remains the same.

It's the fastest way to see how this works in practice.

items.title: Reclaim Your Digital Life: Timelinize Unifies All Your Memories Into One Self-Hosted Story
items.author: GitHubOpenSource
items.content:
Timelinize is a self-hosted application that consolidates data from various accounts and devices into a single, cohesive personal timeline. It allows users to import and organize photos, videos, messages, location history, social media content, and more, providing a comprehensive view of their digital life stored securely on their own computer.

  • вң… Timelinize unifies diverse data types (photos, chats, location history, social media) into a single, cohesive, chronological timeline.

  • вң… The project is self-hosted, ensuring complete data ownership and privacy for all your personal digital memories.

  • вң… It uses advanced features like entity recognition and contextual association to link non-geolocated data to specific times and places.

  • вң… Developers benefit from exploring complex data ingestion, schema normalization, and powerful visualization techniques, including interactive 3D maps.

  • вң… It serves as a robust, open-source alternative to fragmented proprietary cloud services for digital archiving.

    Are your digital memories scattered across a dozen cloud services, old hard drives, and forgotten chat logs? If youвҖҷre like most developers, managing your personal digital history feels like an impossible archiving task. We rely on big tech companies to hold our most precious dataвҖ”photos, messages, location trailsвҖ”but what if you could take all that data back, unify it, and visualize your entire life story in one powerful, self-hosted application? Meet Timelinize, the open-source project designed to give you true sovereignty over your personal data.

    Timelinize isn't just another photo organizer; it's a comprehensive digital historian. Its core purpose is to ingest data from practically every source imaginableвҖ”your phone backups, social media exports, GPS logs, chat archives, and local filesвҖ”and stitch them together into a single, cohesive timeline. This unification process is where the magic happens. By normalizing diverse data types, Timelinize creates a unified view of your life, chronologically and contextually.

    The architecture is designed for massive personal datasets and runs entirely on your own computer. This means you maintain complete ownership and control, eliminating reliance on third-party servers. The project leverages smart data processing to go beyond simple file sorting. For instance, it features robust entity recognition, allowing you to filter your timeline based on specific people, places, or organizations. Even more impressive is its ability to associate non-geolocated data (like a text message) with a location based on contextual clues, such as nearby GPS history, placing that memory accurately on the map.

    For developers, Timelinize presents an incredibly exciting opportunity. It tackles complex challenges related to data ingestion, schema normalization, and advanced visualization. The powerful UI includes features like interactive 3D maps where you can literally walk through your past, seeing memories pinned exactly where they occurred. This project demonstrates how to build sophisticated, privacy-focused tooling for personal data management. If you are interested in data modeling, high-performance local processing (likely powered by Go, judging by the badges), or building stunning, data-rich interfaces, exploring the Timelinize codebase is a must. Contributing to new data importers or enhancing the visualization engine would be a fantastic way to contribute to the future of data ownership. Stop letting your life story be held hostage by fragmented cloud servicesвҖ”it's time to build your own definitive history.

items.title: Release 0.4: My Plan for Contributions to Open Source
items.author: ElshadHu
items.content:
For release 0.4, I decided to explore three things I kept hearing about but never really took the time to understand properly. The plan was to dive into Drizzle ORM, start contributing to Go codebases, and figure out what is MCP. I picked three different repositories to work with:

I chose Drizzle because a lot of developers use it for handling database schemas and queries instead of writing raw SQL everywhere. The main reason I got interested because of my curiosity to databases. ORM stands for Object Relational Mapping. Basically it maps your code objects to database tables. Setting up was straightforward. I activated Docker, cloned the repo, and started reading through the codebase. The architecture is pretty lightweight compared to other ORMs I've seen, which is probably why people like it. What I found interesting is how Drizzle lets you define database schemas in TypeScript instead of writing SQL queries scattered throughout your code. I'm planning to make some pull requests to Drizzle in the future. I want to understand how ORMs actually work under the hood. Contributing seems like the best way to learn that.

Streamplace is a project written mainly in Go and TypeScript. It's a platform for live streaming and video processing - basically the infrastructure that handles live video streams. The backend performance-critical parts are in Go, and the more dynamic parts are in TypeScript. I got interested in this project because of how the code is organized. Coming from TypeScript projects where things can get complex because of high abstraction, seeing a well-structured Go codebase is really refreshing to me. I'll write more about the specific contribution in my next blog post.

Tableau-MCP is a project that makes it easier for developers to work with LLMs while building their projects. It provides resources and tools specifically designed for integrating AI models into your development workflow. The project is still in active development, which is actually why I wanted to contribute to it. It's not in the last stages, it's being built right now. I've been exploring the codebase and learning about MCP (Model Context Protocol). It's a protocol for how applications interact with AI models. I'm planning to make contributions to this project. I'll share the details in my next blog post.

items.title: LiteLLM Broke at 300 RPS in Production. Here's How We Fixed It
items.author: Debby McKinney
items.content:
Tuesday, 2:47 PM Our customer support chatbot is handling 280 RPS. EverythingвҖҷs fine.

2:53 PM Traffic hits 310 RPS. Response times spike. Users start complaining in Slack.

2:58 PM P99 latency reaches 18 seconds. Some requests time out completely.

3:05 PM We manually restart LiteLLM. Traffic drops during the restart. Users are angry.

This happened three times that week.

What We Thought the Problem Was

  • вҖңMaybe we need more replicasвҖқ
  • вҖңLetвҖҷs add a load balancerвҖқ
  • вҖңProbably need better hardwareвҖқ

We scaled horizontally and added three more LiteLLM instances.

  • All instances struggled simultaneously

What the Problem Actually Was

LiteLLM is built on Python + FastAPI.

At low traffic (< 200 RPS), it works well. Past 300 RPS, PythonвҖҷs architecture becomes the bottleneck.

GIL (Global Interpreter Lock): Only one thread executes Python code at a time

  • Async overhead: Event loop coordination adds latency
  • Memory pressure: Heavy dependencies + long-running processes
  • GC pauses: Garbage collection freezes request handling

What We Observed at 350 RPS (Single Instance)

CPU: 85% (one core maxed due to GIL)

  • Memory: 3.2 GB вҶ’ 5.1 GB вҶ’ 6.8 GB (steadily climbing)
  • Latency: 200 ms вҶ’ 2 s вҶ’ 12 s вҶ’ timeout
  • GC pauses: 100вҖ“300 ms every ~30 seconds

After 2 hours, memory reached 8 GB. The process was killed by the OOM killer.

This isnвҖҷt a LiteLLM-specific issue. ItвҖҷs Python hitting its limits at high throughput.

We needed production-grade infrastructure, not a prototype that breaks under load.

So we built in Go, specifically for high-throughput LLM workloads. ItвҖҷs open source and MIT licensed.

Key Architectural Differences

  1. True Concurrency (No GIL)

GoвҖҷs goroutines execute in parallel across all CPU cores.

// Thousands of goroutines, truly parallel go handleRequest(req1) go handleRequest(req3)

// All executing simultaneously

`Go: 10,000 goroutines вүҲ ~100 MB memory

Python: 10,000 threads / async tasks вҶ’ out of memory`

  • GoвҖҷs garbage collector is designed for low-latency systems:
  • Concurrent GC (doesnвҖҷt stop the world)
  • Predictable pause times (typically < 1 ms)
  • No circular-reference memory leaks
  • The Real-World Difference

We ran the same production workload through both gateways.

Test: Customer support chatbot, real user traffic Load: 500 RPS sustained

(3 Г— t3.xlarge instances)

P50 latency: 2.1 s P99 latency: 23.4 s

Memory per instance: 4вҖ“7 GB (climbing)

Timeout rate: 8% Stability: Restart required every 6вҖ“8 hours

P50 latency: 230 ms P99 latency: 520 ms Timeout rate: 0.1% Stability: 30+ days without restart

    But Bifrost IsnвҖҷt Just About Performance

    Rebuilding from scratch let us add production features LiteLLM doesnвҖҷt have.

    1. Adaptive Load Balancing

    Multiple API keys? Bifrost continuously monitors:

    • Traffic is automatically reweighted:

    Real-time weight adjustment: в”ңв”Җ Key 1: 1.2Г— weight (healthy)

    в”ңв”Җ Key 2: 0.5Г— weight (high latency)

    в””в”Җ Key 3: 1.0Г— weight (normal)

    No manual intervention required.

    Not exact-match caching вҖ” semantic similarity.

    вҖңHow do I reset my password?вҖқ

    вҖңWhatвҖҷs the password reset process?вҖқ

    The second query hits the cache.

    • Cost savings: ~$1,200/month

    3. Zero-Overhead Observability

    Every request is logged with full context:

      All async. Zero performance impact. Built-in dashboard.

      4. Production-Grade Failover

      Primary provider down? Bifrost automatically fails over.

      WeвҖҷve had OpenAI incidents where traffic switched to Anthropic automatically. Users didnвҖҷt notice.

      Migration Was Surprisingly Easy

      Expected: Days of refactoring Actual: ~15 minutes

      : Start Bifrost docker run -p 8080:8080 -v $(pwd)/data:/app/data maximhq/bifrost

      Visit:

      : Change One Line in Code

      import openai openai.api_key = "sk-..."

      import openai openai.api_base = "http://localhost:8080/openai"

      openai.api_key = "sk-..."

      is OpenAI-compatible. If your code works with OpenAI, it works with Bifrost.

      Supports LangChain, LlamaIndex, LiteLLM SDK, and more.

      Shut down 2 of 3 LiteLLM instances

      Performance better than ever

      Three Months Later, Zero downtime incidents

      Handling 800+ RPS during peaks

      Monthly cost: $60 vs $450

      Use if:

      • You need deep Python ecosystem integration
      • YouвҖҷre okay with manual scaling and monitoring
      • YouвҖҷre running production workloads
      • Traffic > 200 RPS (or will be soon)
      • You care about P99 latency
      • You want predictable costs
      • YouвҖҷre tired of restarting your gateway

      Try Bifrost; Open source (MIT). Run it locally in 30 seconds:

      git clone https://github.com/maximhq/bifrost cd bifrost

      Benchmark It Yourself cd bifrost/benchmarks

      ./benchmark -provider bifrost -rate 500 -duration 60

      Compare with your current setup.

      LiteLLM breaking at ~300 RPS wasnвҖҷt a bug. It was Python hitting its architectural limits.

      We needed production-grade infrastructure. So we built it вҖ” in Go вҖ” and open sourced it.

      If youвҖҷre hitting scale issues with your LLM gateway, youвҖҷre not alone. We hit them too.

      Bifrost solved them. Might solve yours.

      Built by the team at Maxim AI. We also build evaluation and observability tools for production AI systems.

items.title: Built an Audit System in a Single 15MB Binary That Even DBAs Can't Fool
items.author: Anes1032
items.content:
When auditors ask you to prove your data hasn't been tampered with, what do you show them?

Access logs? Backups? pgaudit output?

But what if the DBA who generated those logs is the one committing fraud? How would you detect that?

DBAs are gods (Superusers). They have the power to modify data and erase the evidence. "We just have to trust the admins" вҖ” is that really acceptable?

I built an OSS called to answer this question: No Kafka, no dedicated DB, no additional servers, no complex configuration вҖ” just a single 15MB binary.

Here's what happens when an attacker tries to tamper with data that should never change:

Witnz monitors PostgreSQL's transaction log (WAL) externally and unauthorized changes вҖ” regardless of who made them.

Comparison with Other Solutions

Yes (full DB replacement)
Yes (full DB replacement)
  • pgaudit only logs queries; Witnz actively verifies data integrity
  • pgaudit logs can be tampered; Witnz uses distributed hash chains
  • Witnz provides real-time alerts on tampering
  • No migration required - works with existing PostgreSQL
  • Same trust model (see Security Considerations below)
  • Significantly lower deployment cost
  • 1000x lighter (~15MB vs multi-GB infrastructure)
  • Hours to deploy vs weeks/months

Witnz delivers a blockchain-like trust model with the simplicity of a sidecar you can drop next to your app servers.

Why Can It Detect DBA Fraud?

The key is monitoring from outside the DB and locking evidence via distributed consensus.

Layer 1: Real-time WAL Monitoring (Instant)

  • Receives change events via PostgreSQL Logical Replication
  • / and alerts
  • Even if the DBA deletes logs, Witnz has already captured the WAL

Layer 2: Merkle Root Verification (Periodic, Fast)

  • Periodically fetches all records in and computes Merkle Root
  • Compares against stored Merkle Root Checkpoint
  • Catches tampering that bypasses Logical Replication:
    • Direct DB file manipulation
    • Manual SQL during node downtime
    • Restore from tampered backups
    • Phantom inserts via unmonitored methods

Distributed Consensus for Tamper Resistance

  • (3+ nodes recommended, works with 1)
  • Nodes share "the correct DB state" (Hash Chain + Merkle Root)
  • : Evidence stored locally, zero external DB dependency

Even if a DBA tampers with the DB, it won't match the "ground truth" held by the Witnz cluster вҖ” and gets caught immediately.

Raft consensus operates on a feudal-style system with the Leader as sovereign, lacking mechanisms to prevent tampering by the Leader node. вҶ’ This will be addressed through monitoring by Witness nodes, as described below.

Tech Stack: Simplicity First 

- Language: Go (easy cross-compilation)
- DB Integration: PostgreSQL Logical Replication (jackc/pglogrepl)
- Consensus: Raft (hashicorp/raft)
- Storage: BoltDB (etcd-io/bbolt)
- Hashing: SHA256 + Merkle Tree
- Binary Size: ~15MB

Zero additional infrastructure. No Kafka, no dedicated DB, no Java VM.

Protection Mode: For Append-Only Tables

Witnz is designed for like audit logs and transaction histories.

What Attacks Can It Detect?

Direct DB file manipulation
Tampering during node downtime
Restore Tampered DB backup

Getting Started (Single Node)

1. Enable Logical Replication in PostgreSQL 


curl  https://github.com/Anes1032/witnz/releases/latest/download/witnz-linux-amd64  /usr/local/bin/witnz
 +x /usr/local/bin/witnz

witnz init  witnz.yaml
witnz start  witnz.yaml

A scalable audit system running from a single 15MB binary.

git clone https://github.com/Anes1032/witnz.git
witnz
docker-compose up

Three Witnz nodes spin up and start monitoring PostgreSQL.

  • Append-only mode with real-time UPDATE/DELETE detection
  • Merkle Root verification with specific tampered record identification
  • 3-node Raft cluster with automatic failover
  • PostgreSQL Logical Replication integration
  • Multi-platform support (Linux, macOS, Windows)

Witnz has a fundamental limitation: if a Raft leader node is compromised with , it can submit false hash values that followers will accept.

  • Root access to the leader node's server
  • Ability to modify the running binary or restart with a tampered version

The Same Applies to Other Solutions

Attacker can submit false hashes
Attacker can submit false data and proofs
Attacker with AWS access can manipulate
Root access = full control

No software-only solution can protect against server root compromise. This is a fundamental limitation shared by all database integrity tools, including immudb.

The only theoretical protection is hardware-based root of trust (TPM, AWS Nitro Enclave, Intel SGX), which requires trusting the hardware vendor.

What Witnz Protects Against

  • Database administrator misconduct (without server root access)
  • SQL injection attacks modifying audit records
  • Direct database file tampering (detected via Merkle Root)
  • Application-level bugs causing unauthorized modifications

items.title: Go From Zero to Depth вҖ” Part 3: Stack vs Heap & How Escape Analysis Actually Works
items.author: Pavel Sanikovich
items.content:
If youвҖҷve ever profiled a Go program and wondered why a simple function allocates memory, or why a tiny struct suddenly ends up on the heap, youвҖҷve seen the effects of escape analysis. Beginners often learn stack and heap as if they were fixed rules вҖ” small things go on the stack, large things go on the heap вҖ” but Go doesnвҖҷt work like that at all. Size is irrelevant. What matters is lifetime.

A value stays on the stack only if the compiler can prove it never outlives the function that created it. The moment the lifetime becomes ambiguous, that value вҖңescapes,вҖқ and Go places it on the heap. This is not a heuristic and not guesswork; it is a strict safety rule.

Understanding this rule gives you x-ray vision into your Go programs. You start predicting allocations before they happen. You see how small changes in code shape memory behavior. And most importantly, you learn to write Go the way the compiler expects вҖ” which results in faster, cleaner, more predictable programs.

Let's walk through this from the ground up.

The Stack: Fast, Local, Temporary

A stack frame exists only during the execution of a function. When the function returns, the frame disappears. If a value can be proven to stay within that frame, it is stack allocated.

If we ask the compiler to show escape analysis:

$ go build -gcflags="-m"
<no escape>

Nothing escapes. Everything is on the stack. The compiler is even free to inline the function, meaning the variables may never exist as вҖңvariablesвҖқ at all вҖ” they become registers or constants.

This is the ideal path: pure stack behavior, no GC pressure, no heap work.

The Heap: For Values with Extended Lifetime

A value must live on the heap if something outside the current stack frame needs to reference it. Returning a pointer is the most common example:

./main.go:4:9: &x escapes to heap

This has nothing to do with the size of . The compiler simply sees that the caller needs a reference to after the function returns. The stack frame cannot hold it anymore.

A beginner often doesnвҖҷt realize that the is not expensive вҖ” itвҖҷs the lifetime extension that forces the escape.

Closures: When Variables Quietly Escape

Closures are a classic place where beginners accidentally create heap allocations.

./main.go:6:13: func literal escapes to heap
./main.go:5:5: moved to heap: x

Why? Because the returned function continues to exist after finishes. It needs access to . Therefore, must move to the heap, where its lifetime is no longer tied to the stack frame.

Many beginners write closure-based code without realizing they are allocating memory every time.

Two Constructors, Two Lifetimes, Two Allocation Patterns

This is one of the clearest ways to see escape analysis in action.

Version 1 вҖ” returning a value:

And now version 2 вҖ” returning a pointer:

For the first version, the compiler often places directly into the callerвҖҷs stack frame. For the pointer version, the allocation must occur on the heap.

Same data. Same fields. Same size. Different lifetime = different memory behavior.

This is why experienced Go developers say: prefer returning values unless you need shared mutable state.

Escape Analysis Loves Clear Ownership

HereвҖҷs a subtle example where a tiny rewrite prevents a heap escape:

./main.go:7:12: &total escapes to heap

But if we write it like this:

Exact same logic. Different lifetime semantics.

This is the power of understanding escape analysis: your intuition becomes aligned with the compiler.

A Surprising Case: Heap Allocations Without Pointers

Sometimes a heap escape happens even though you donвҖҷt return a pointer. A classic example:

./main.go:5:10: func literal escapes to heap
./main.go:4:6: moved to heap: i

Why? Because the goroutine runs after the loop iteration completes. It needs access to . Therefore cannot live on the stack.

This is the precise moment when a beginner realizes that concurrency also changes lifetimes.

What Escape Analysis Is Actually Doing

ItвҖҷs not trying to optimize your code. ItвҖҷs proving safety.

If the compiler can prove a value is local вҶ’ stack. If it cannot prove вҶ’ heap.

ItвҖҷs a conservative algorithm. A value will escape even when theoretically safe, simply because proving otherwise would require solving undecidable problems. Go plays it safe; thatвҖҷs what keeps programs correct.

How to See Escape Analysis in Your Code

You can observe everything the compiler decides:

go build -gcflags="-m -m"

This becomes addictive. You begin scanning your code and escapes before the compiler prints them.

Once you reach that level, Go feels like a language that explains itself to you.

Why This Matters for Beginners

Understanding escape analysis is not about premature optimization. ItвҖҷs about forming the mental model that Go expects you to have.

Once you understand lifetimes:

вҖ“ you choose value vs pointer intentionally вҖ“ you design APIs that minimize hidden allocations

вҖ“ your code scales better under load

вҖ“ you avoid concurrency pitfalls

вҖ“ you become predictable to the compiler

This is exactly where beginners stop being beginners.

Next: Part 4 вҖ” Pointers in Go, Without Fear

In the next chapter weвҖҷll explore pointers, not as something вҖңlow-level,вҖқ but as the mechanism that shapes ownership and lifetime in Go. WeвҖҷll explain why pointers are often misunderstood, why they donвҖҷt work like C pointers, and why the value/pointer distinction is the foundation of GoвҖҷs design.

Memory model вҶ’ escape analysis вҶ’ pointers вҶ’ concurrency вҶ’ scheduler YouвҖҷre now in the deep part of the pool.

This series focuses on , not just using it.

If you want to continue in the same mindset,

is a great next step.

ItвҖҷs a single subscription that gives you access to hundreds of in-depth, text-based courses вҖ” from Go internals and concurrency to system design and distributed systems. No videos, no per-course purchases, just structured learning you can move through at your own pace.

items.title: DevPill 10 - Fault tolerance: adding retries to your Go code
items.author: Raul Paes Silva
items.content:
Adding retries to your API is a must to make your system more resilient. You can add them in database operations, communication with external apis and every other operation that might depend on a response of a third party.

Here's an example of a retry implementation for database transient errors:

1. First, let's code the retry function

The function receives the number of attempts, delay (interval between attempts) and the function which represents the operation you want to do.

2. IsTransientError function

In this example I created a function that check if the operation is worth trying again based on the error. Let's say the repository returns "sql.ErrNoRows", which is returned when the query indicates no rows were found for the select command. This error definitely does not need to be attempted again. That's why we're looking for errors that are transient, that might happen for a few milliseconds/seconds for network reasons for example.

items.title: Building a Shader Compiler in Pure Go: naga Reaches v0.4.0
items.author: Andrey Kolkov
items.content:
A few weeks ago, I published GoGPU: A Pure Go Graphics Library for GPU Programming вҖ” introducing an ecosystem for GPU computing in Go without CGO. Today, I want to share progress on one of the most challenging components: , a shader compiler written entirely in Go.

compiles WGSL (WebGPU Shading Language) to SPIR-V bytecode. 17,000 lines of pure Go. No CGO. No external dependencies. Just .

  • now supports compute shaders with atomics and barriers
  • Full type inference system
  • Production-ready for graphics and compute workloads

Why Build a Shader Compiler?

If you've worked with GPU programming in Go, you've hit this wall: shader compilation always requires external tools. Rust's , Google's , or NVIDIA's toolchain. That means:

  • Platform-specific binaries

The GoGPU ecosystem aims to eliminate this friction. A Pure Go shader compiler means:

go build ./...

The Journey: v0.1.0 to v0.4.0

v0.1.0 вҖ” Foundation (~10K LOC)

  • WGSL lexer recognizing 140+ tokens
  • Intermediate representation (33 expression types, 16 statement types)
  • SPIR-V binary writer with 100+ opcodes

Result: vertex and fragment shaders compiled successfully.

v0.2.0 вҖ” Type System (~2K LOC)

The hard part: type inference. SPIR-V requires explicit types for everything, but WGSL allows inference:

let x = 1.0;           // What type? f32
let v = vec3(1.0);     // vec3<f32>
let n = normalize(v);  // Also vec3<f32>, inferred from function return

Built a complete type resolution engine that tracks types through every expression.

v0.3.0 вҖ” Textures (~3K LOC)

Added texture operations вҖ” the bread and butter of graphics:

@fragment
fn main(@location(0) uv: vec2<f32>) -> @location(0) vec4<f32> {
    return textureSample(myTexture, mySampler, uv);
}

This required implementing SPIR-V image operations: , , and friends.

v0.4.0 вҖ” Compute Shaders (~2K LOC)

The latest release brings GPU compute capabilities:

@group(0) @binding(0)
var<storage, read_write> counter: atomic<u32>;

@compute @workgroup_size(64)
fn main(@builtin(global_invocation_id) id: vec3<u32>) {
    atomicAdd(&counter, 1u);
    workgroupBarrier();
}
  • Storage buffer access modes: ,
  • :
  • : add, sub, min, max, and, or, xor, exchange, compare-exchange
  • : workgroup, storage, texture
  • : for atomic pointers

в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
