items.title: How I Designed a Zero-Waste, Event-Driven Analytics Pipeline Using DynamoDB TTL and AWS Lambda
items.author: Pedro Oliveira
items.content:
It is time to get the data Barx is generating and bring it to the barbers. My first option was to create a cron job that runs every Monday at 3 am UTC. When my lambda triggers, I search for all barbershops in DynamoDB and start the analytics calculation for each one.

However, this approach presents some resource and cost challenges:

  1. Scanning the entire DynamoDB table is resource-intensive and can be costly.
  2. A single lambda function handles a heavy workload, which may impact performance and scalability.
  3. Every Monday, the process attempts to retrieve all barbershops, regardless of their activity status.
  4. Analytics calculations are performed for all barbershops, including those without any activity in the previous week, leading to unnecessary resource consumption.

Probably there are more problems, but I won't ask for AI to make it more dramatic...

The point is, I would like to be able to spend resources only with those barbershops that had activities from last week and recalculate their analysis regarding the last 7, 30, 60, and 90 days.

The architecture proposal

Thinking on that, I built this architecture:

It looks simple, but it helps me to schedule a job only for those barbershops that had some activity, so I create a record in DynamoDB to expire next Monday at 3 am UTC.

When the record is deleted because TTL rule, it triggers another lambda that shares the data of that barbershop that was scheduled to start in a dedicated lambda for its analytic calculation.

This approach helps me to spend resources only with users who are producing data, not those who are not producing.

  1. Analytics calculations are performed only for barbershops with recent activity, optimizing resource usage.
  2. Scheduled records in DynamoDB are automatically cleaned up via TTL, reducing manual maintenance.
  3. The architecture scales efficiently, supporting any number of users without additional complexity.
  1. If many records expire at the same time, a large number of lambdas may be triggered simultaneously, leading to a spike in concurrent executions. (But I can handle this, configuring better DynamoDB Stream batch size and parallelization factor).
  2. There may be a delay between the scheduled expiration time and the actual triggering of the lambda, depending on the load on the DynamoDB Streams service. (This is acceptable for my use case since analytics don't need to be real-time).

My intention in posting this article is to share my idea and get feedback from the community. If you have any suggestions for improvement or alternative approaches, please let me know in the comments!

items.title: The Wrong Database Connection: A Go Deadlock Story
items.author: Haru
items.content:
TL;DR: Using the wrong connection within a limited connection pool leads to deadlock when concurrent executions exhaust all available connections.

Imagine you're a software engineer (and you probably are, considering you're reading this). One day you log in at work, check your workload status, your Grafana dashboard, or if you were diligent enough to create proper alerting, you get paged via PagerDuty, only to discover your application has been stuck for minutes, hours, or even days.

Not dead, just stuck in an idle state, unresponsive to any events. Or even worse, some liveness/readiness probes start failing apparently at random, causing restarts of your service and leaving you with few insights to debug. And in the case no proper alerting or monitoring is set, you won't easily detect this.

Unfortunately, I saw this happen in the past and the issue was related to a very subtle problem: wrong use of database connections and transactions within the same connection pool, causing a terrible at the application level.

Let me explain this clearly, with the hope it will save your day in the future. I'll provide examples in Go since it's the language I'm most familiar with, but the concept applies to other languages as well.

Suppose you have a backend application that relies on a PostgreSQL database. Here's the simplest way to connect to a database and set up a connection pool in Go:

Let's focus on the method. According to the documentation:

// SetMaxOpenConns sets the maximum number of open connections to the database.

This means you can have at most open connections from your process to the database (4 in this example). When the maximum number of connections is reached, goroutines will wait until an existing connection is released. Once that happens, the connection is acquired to perform whatever operation is needed.

Let's expand our example by adding concurrent workers that use the connection pool to perform transactional operations against the database:

Some of you may have already spotted something wrong with this code, but in production codebases with layers of wrappers and nested methods, such issues aren't always so clear and evident. Let's continue and see what happens when we run this code.

When we run our code with a number of workers the connection pool size:

2025/08/08 11:53:32 Successfully connected to the db
2025/08/08 11:53:32 Running worker 1
2025/08/08 11:53:32 Running worker 0
2025/08/08 11:53:37 worker 1 ended
2025/08/08 11:53:37 worker 0 ended

Everything works fine. Now let's increase the number of workers to 4 ( the connection pool size):

2025/08/08 11:59:10 Successfully connected to the db
2025/08/08 11:59:10 Running worker 3
2025/08/08 11:59:10 Running worker 0
2025/08/08 11:59:10 Running worker 1
2025/08/08 11:59:10 Running worker 2
2025/08/08 11:59:15 worker 2 ended
2025/08/08 11:59:15 worker 0 ended
2025/08/08 11:59:15 worker 1 ended
2025/08/08 11:59:15 worker 3 ended

Still working fine. Now let's increase the number of workers to the connection pool size:

2025/08/08 12:00:44 Successfully connected to the db
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 9
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 3
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 7
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 4
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 0
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 2
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 8
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 5
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 6
2025/08/08 12:00:44 Running worker 1

No workers ended this timeвҖ”the application entered a .

At this point, what should be the next step to get more insights? In my case, it was using a . You can achieve this in Go by instrumenting your application with pprof. One of the simplest ways to use it is by exposing a web server that serves runtime profiling data:

One interesting thing you can get from pprof, besides CPU and memory profiles, is the full goroutine stack dump by accessing http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=2. This gives you something like:

goroutine 28 [select]:
database/sql.(*DB).conn(0xc000111450, {0x866310, 0xb10e20}, 0x1)
    /usr/local/go/src/database/sql/sql.go:1369 +0x425
database/sql.(*DB).exec(0xc000111450, {0x866310, 0xb10e20}, {0x7f09d8, 0x4f}, {0xc000075f10, 0x4, 0x4}, 0xbe?)
    /usr/local/go/src/database/sql/sql.go:1689 +0x54
// ... more stack trace

goroutine 33 [chan receive]:
database/sql.(*Tx).awaitDone(0xc00025e000)
    /usr/local/go/src/database/sql/sql.go:2212 +0x29
created by database/sql.(*DB).beginDC in goroutine 28

goroutine 51 [chan receive]:
database/sql.(*Tx).awaitDone(0xc0000b0100)
    /usr/local/go/src/database/sql/sql.go:2212 +0x29
created by database/sql.(*DB).beginDC in goroutine 25
// ... omitting the full dump for readability

By inspecting the dump more carefully, we can see the evidence of the problem:

goroutine 19 [select]: database/sql.(*DB).conn() // Waiting for connection
goroutine 20 [select]: database/sql.(*DB).conn() // Waiting for connection  
goroutine 21 [select]: database/sql.(*DB).conn() // Waiting for connection
// ... and so on

The statement is a control structure that lets a goroutine wait on multiple communication operations. Meanwhile, other goroutines are holding active transactions:

goroutine 33 [chan receive]: database/sql.(*Tx).awaitDone() // Active transaction
goroutine 51 [chan receive]: database/sql.(*Tx).awaitDone() // Active transaction
// etc.

The goroutines are transaction monitors that wait for the transaction to be committed, rolled back, or canceledвҖ”they're doing their job correctly.

What we have is a where all available database connections are held by transactions that aren't progressing, while other goroutines indefinitely wait for those same resources.

Let's examine our worker code again, focusing on this critical part:

  1. Beginning a transaction, which acquires a connection from the pool
  2. Using the client to execute a query, which tries to acquire connection
  3. Committing or rolling back based on the operation status

The problem is that using the client after creating a transaction results in . Here's exactly how the deadlock occurs:

  • Worker 0 begins a transaction вҶ’ acquires connection 1 (Pool: 1/4 used)
  • Worker 1 begins a transaction вҶ’ acquires connection 2 (Pool: 2/4 used)
  • Worker 2 begins a transaction вҶ’ acquires connection 3 (Pool: 3/4 used)
  • Worker 3 begins a transaction вҶ’ acquires connection 4 (Pool: 4/4 used)
  • Worker 0 calls вҶ’ tries to acquire connection 5, but pool is exhausted
  • Worker 1 calls вҶ’ tries to acquire connection 6, but pool is exhausted
  • Worker 2 calls вҶ’ tries to acquire connection 7, but pool is exhausted
  • Worker 3 calls вҶ’ tries to acquire connection 8, but pool is exhausted
  • Everyone is waiting for connections that will never be released.

The issue causing all this trouble is the wrong use of instead of . The correct way is to use the transaction handle, which uses the same connection that the transaction already holds:

It's remarkable how two characters ( vs ) can halt your entire production system. While this may seem simple to spot in this example, in large production codebases it can be much harder to detect. In my case, it was randomly affecting one pod in our Kubernetes deployment at unpredictable times, especially during high load and concurrency spikes.

How can you avoid this? I see several alternatives:

1. Proper Concurrency Testing

Test your application under realistic concurrent load to spot such issues before production.

Structure your code so you can't accidentally open new connections within a transactional block.

3. Use pgxpool for Better Control

For Go applications, consider using

pgxpool which provides more granular control over the connection pool. The explicit / pattern makes it much clearer when you're using connections:

4. Monitoring and Alerting

I intentionally avoid mentioning palliative measures like connection timeouts, which may mask the underlying issue while causing performance degradation. Instead, implement proper liveness/readiness probes paired with alerts on restart frequency. This provides a good tradeoff between keeping the system running and being notified when something isn't behaving correctly.

  • Test under realistic concurrent load
  • Be consistent in resource usage (always be aware on how connections usage happens)
  • Use the right tool, like pprof, to support your debugging against mysterious hangs

items.title: Your Primary LLM Provider Failed? Enable Automatic Fallback with Bifrost
items.author: Debby McKinney
items.content:
When building applications that depend on external LLM APIs, provider failures are inevitable. Network timeouts, rate limits, model unavailability, and service outages all cause request failures that break application functionality. This post explains how to implement automatic fallback mechanisms using Bifrost to maintain service availability when primary providers fail.

Failure Scenarios in LLM Applications

LLM API calls can fail for several reasons:

  • Rate limiting: HTTP 429 responses when quota limits are exceeded
  • Network errors: Timeouts, DNS failures, connection refused
  • Provider outages: HTTP 500/502/503/504 server errors
  • Model unavailability: Specific models offline for maintenance
  • Authentication issues: Invalid API keys or expired tokens
  • Regional restrictions: Geo-blocking or service unavailability

Applications typically handle these failures by showing error messages to users or failing silently. Both approaches degrade user experience and system reliability.

Automatic Fallback Architecture

Bifrost implements automatic failover by maintaining an ordered list of provider configurations. When a request fails, the system attempts each fallback provider sequentially until one succeeds or all providers are exhausted.

  1. Primary Request: Execute request against primary provider
  2. Retry Logic: If request fails with retryable status codes (500, 502, 503, 504, 429), retry the same provider
  3. Fallback Execution: After all retries are exhausted, attempt next provider in fallback list
  4. Plugin Re-execution: Run all configured plugins for each fallback attempt
  5. Response Handling: Return successful response or original error if all providers fail

Fallbacks are configured by adding a fallbacks array to the request payload:


curl  POST http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions

The system attempts providers in this order:

  1. openai/gpt-4o-mini (primary)
  2. anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 (first fallback)
  3. bedrock/anthropic.claude-3-sonnet-20240229-v1:0 (second fallback)

Responses maintain standard OpenAI API compatibility regardless of which provider handled the request:

The extra_fields.provider indicates which provider actually processed the request, enabling monitoring and analytics. Note that latency metrics (when available) will be provided in milliseconds.

The system uses two separate mechanisms for handling failures: retries and fallbacks.

Retries are configured at each provider level and occur before attempting fallbacks. The system allows retries for these specific status codes:

  • HTTP 500: Internal Server Error
  • HTTP 503: Service Unavailable
  • HTTP 504: Gateway Timeout
  • HTTP 429: Too Many Requests

When a request fails with a retryable status code, the system will retry the same provider multiple times before moving to fallbacks.

Fallbacks (Cross-Provider)

Fallbacks are attempted after all retries for a provider have been exhausted. Unlike retries, fallbacks are allowed for any failure type because response status from provider A should not impact fallback attempts on provider B.

However, plugins can prevent fallback execution using the AllowFallbacks field on BifrostError. For example, an authentication plugin can block all fallbacks and return the error immediately if there's a fundamental auth issue that would affect all providers.

Plugin Execution Behavior

Each fallback attempt is treated as a new request, triggering complete plugin re-execution:

  • Semantic caching: Cache lookups run against each provider's cache
  • Governance rules: Rate limits and content policies apply per provider
  • Logging: Each attempt generates separate log entries
  • Monitoring: Metrics track attempts per provider

This ensures consistent behavior regardless of which provider handles the final request.

Plugins can prevent fallback execution by setting the AllowFallbacks field on BifrostError. This provides fine-grained control over when fallbacks should be attempted:

When a plugin sets AllowFallbacks=False, the system immediately returns the original error without attempting any fallbacks, even if they are configured.

For more details on plugin fallback control, see the Bifrost documentation.

curl  POST http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions 
curl  POST http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions 
curl  POST http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions

Monitoring and Observability

Monitor which providers handle requests using the provider field in responses:

  • Fallback trigger rate per provider
  • Success rate by provider position
  • Average latency per provider (when available)

Monitor request flow through the retry and fallback process:

  • Increased latency when fallbacks are triggered
  • Higher complexity in request tracing and debugging
  • Potential cost increases from using multiple providers
  • Model response variations between providers may affect application behavior

Automatic fallbacks provide a systematic approach to handling LLM provider failures without requiring application code changes. By configuring multiple providers and letting the system handle failover logic, applications can maintain availability during provider outages, rate limiting, and other failure scenarios.

The key is proper failure classification, comprehensive monitoring, and thoughtful provider selection to balance reliability, cost, and performance requirements.

items.title: Introducing GoCVKit: Zero-Boilerplate Computer Vision in Go
items.author: Elliot Silver
items.content:
Hey there, fellow Gophers! If youвҖҷve worked with computer vision in Go, you know GoCV is fantastic for accessing OpenCVвҖҷs power.

But the reality? Boilerplate everywhere: camera setup, Mat management, window handling, resource leaks, and recompiling just to tweak a parameter. ItвҖҷs not exactly вҖңfun.вҖқ

ThatвҖҷs why I created GoCVKitвҖ”a modular framework that makes real-time CV prototyping smooth, efficient, and genuinely enjoyable.

GoCVKit is a clean, idiomatic layer on top of GoCV for live camera or video streams. It handles the heavy lifting so you can focus on ideas, not plumbing.

вҖў Zero boilerplate: Full apps in вүӨ10 lines.

вҖў Hot-reload config: Edit config.toml and changes apply instantlyвҖ”no restarts.

вҖў Performance-focused: Double-buffered pipelines with zero per-frame allocations.

вҖў Extensible: Built-in processors (Grayscale, GaussianBlur, Canny, Sobel, etc.) plus easy custom filters.

вҖў Quality-of-life extras: Video recording, toggleable FPS overlay, frame callbacks, graceful shutdown, and seamless input switching (webcam or file).

Go is perfect for CV: fast, concurrent, and easy to deploy. But raw GoCV meant rewriting the same scaffolding repeatedly. GoCVKit eliminates that pain, making it ideal for: вҖў Teaching and demos вҖў Real-time vision apps

вҖў Anyone who wants to stay sane while experimenting

go get github.com/Elliot727/gocvkit

Head to the repo for full documentation, processor list, and custom filter guides: github.com/Elliot727/gocvkit

Star it if it saves you time, contribute if you make it better, and tell your friendsвҖ”Go deserves a first-class CV experience! рҹҡҖ What real-time CV projects are you working on? Let me know in the comments!

items.title: Go From Zero to Depth вҖ” Part 2: Go Memory Model Explained Simply (But Correctly)
items.author: Pavel Sanikovich
items.content:
Beginners usually hear a reassuring story about memory in Go: small things live on the stack, large things live on the heap, the garbage collector handles cleanup, and escape analysis is a mysterious optimization pass that decides where everything goes. This story is simple, but it hides the actual forces that shape how Go works. The reality is both subtler and more interesting, and once you see it, the way you write Go code changes forever.

The right way to understand GoвҖҷs memory model is to forget about stack and heap for a moment and focus instead on the nature of values. Go is a value-oriented language. Everything starts with a value: an integer, a struct, a slice header, a map handle. The crucial question is not вҖңwhereвҖқ the value lives but вҖңhow longвҖқ it needs to live. Lifetime, not size, governs GoвҖҷs memory behavior.

A stack frame lives only as long as a function call. If a value can stay within that frame safely, it will. That decision is the essence of escape analysis. Nothing mystical is happening: the compiler simply looks at each value and decides whether it can remain tied to the call stack or whether something forces it to survive beyond the function that created it.

To see how straightforward this is, consider the simplest possible example:

If you ask Go to print escape analysis results, you will see nothing dramatic:

$ go build -gcflags="-m"
<no escape>

The compiler sees that never leaves this function except as a copy. It can happily live on the stack.

But now make a tiny change:

./main.go:4:9: &x escapes to heap

The value must now outlive the stack frame of , because the caller receives a pointer to it. Go cannot keep on the stack anymore. Nothing about the size or complexity changed. Only the lifetime changed. The moment you return a pointer, your value escapes.

This is where beginners usually pause. Returning a pointer feels harmless, even idiomatic, yet it changes how memory is managed. This is why thinking in terms of ownership is more important than memorizing stack vs heap rules. A pointer implies shared ownership. A value implies independent ownership. The compiler simply enforces this distinction.

Closures reveal this idea even more clearly. Consider this common pattern:

Here is referenced by a function literal that survives after returns. Go performs the only correct action:

./main.go:6:13: func literal escapes to heap
./main.go:5:5: moved to heap: x

When you run the program, every call increments the same , and that would be impossible if had stayed on the stack. The heap is not a performance penalty here; it is the correct expression of the variableвҖҷs lifetime.

Not all escapes are required. Some are accidental. A lot of performance issues in Go come from patterns that unknowingly extend lifetimes.

Compare two versions of a constructor:

In the first case, is returned by value. The compiler can often avoid heap allocation entirely by placing the struct directly in the callerвҖҷs stack frame. In the second case, returning a pointer forces escape. A beginner may prefer pointer semantics because they вҖңfeel cleaner,вҖқ but the cost appears only when you look deeper.

The same applies to slices and maps. A slice header is a small struct containing a pointer to underlying data. Returning a slice does not necessarily cause a heap escape; returning a pointer to part of a slice almost always does. Ownership and lifetime drive everything.

The garbage collector fits neatly into this picture. It is not sweeping memory randomly; it is following chains of references established by your program. If nothing points to a value, the value dies. If something still points to it, the value lives. This makes clarity of ownership a performance feature. The simpler your reference graph, the less work the GC performs.

Understanding all of this does not require deep systems knowledge. It requires only the willingness to look under the surface and recognize that GoвҖҷs model is not magical. It is logical. And once you see the logic, your intuition sharpens. You start predicting when a value will escape. You notice unnecessary pointers. You understand how closures extend lifetimes. You become aware that Go optimizes for the code you write, not the code you intended.

The Go memory model is approachable because the language removes distractions. There is no manual memory management, no pointer arithmetic, no undefined behavior. What remains is a direct connection between the structure of your program and the shape of its memory. For a beginner, discovering this is often the moment Go stops looking вҖңtoo simpleвҖқ and starts looking deeply intentional.

In the next part, we will examine escape analysis more closely. We will look at stack vs heap behavior in detail, explore how the compiler decides where values go, and demonstrate how small changes in code ripple through performance. If Part 2 revealed the shape of GoвҖҷs memory model, Part 3 will show how to work with it deliberately.

The value of Go lies not in hidden complexity but in transparent mechanisms that reward clear thinking. The memory model is the first place where this becomes unmistakable.

items.title: I built a real-time multiplayer typing game (and shipped 2 features in 5 days based on feedback)
items.author: Nico Hirsch
items.content:
I built TYPECHAMP - a real-time multiplayer typing race game where you compete against friends (or strangers) to see who can type the fastest.

Think TypeRacer meets modern web tech.

  • Next.js 16 + React 19 (with the new React Compiler!)
  • Go 1.25 with WebSocket for real-time communication

The WebSocket connection maintains <100ms latency for real-time cursor position updates, so you can literally see your opponents typing character-by-character.

I launched V1 five days ago on Reddit and got... 2 upvotes. рҹҳ…

But more importantly, I got :

"This is cool but I lack friends :( Can you add public lobbies so I can race strangers?"

Fast Iteration: V1 вҶ’ V2 in 5 Days

Within the next few days, I shipped:

1. Public Lobbies (Dec 5)

  • Added flag to lobby model
  • Built page with auto-refresh (every 5s)
  • REST API endpoint:
  • Players can now browse and join public races

2. Custom Text Passages (Dec 8)

Another user mentioned using it for in India. They needed to practice specific passages.

  • "Custom" as a 4th language option (alongside English/German/French)
  • Textarea for pasting any text (no length limits)
  • Full Unicode support (handles emoji, Chinese characters, German umlauts, etc.)
  • Text persists across multiple rounds for practice

Turned a typing game into a with one feature.

Real-time synchronization:

// Go backend broadcasts to all clients in a lobby
func (h *Hub) BroadcastToLobby(lobbyCode string, message []byte) {
    h.mu.RLock()
    defer h.mu.RUnlock()

    for client := range h.clients {
        if client.LobbyCode == lobbyCode {
            client.send <- message
        }
    }
}

Unicode handling on frontend:
// Use Array.from() instead of .split() for proper multi-byte char support
const chars = Array.from(text); // вң… Works with emoji, ГӨ, Г¶, Гҹ, дёӯж–Ү
const chars = text.split('');   // вқҢ Breaks multi-byte characters
  1. Launch fast, iterate faster - V1 wasn't perfect, but feedback made V2 way better
  2. Users will tell you what they need - Both features came from user requests
  3. Technical choices matter - WebSocket is perfect for this use case
  4. New use cases emerge - Never expected typing exam prep to be a use case!

It's free, no signup required. Create a lobby and share the 6-character code with friends (or join a public lobby).

Would love your feedback - especially from the dev.to community!

  • Product Hunt launch (once I have more traction)
  • Competitive ranked mode with matchmaking (planned for January)
  • Leaderboards and user profiles

Built in public. Shipped fast. Learning as I go.

items.title: Go From Zero to Depth вҖ” Part 1: The Hidden Simplicity of Go
items.author: Pavel Sanikovich
items.content:
When people first open Go, they often react the same way: The language feels almost suspiciously small. No generics (until recently), no inheritance, no complex type systems, no decorators, no macros, no operator overloading, no frameworks built into the standard library. It looks humble, even primitive, especially if you come from ecosystems that pride themselves on gigantic feature sets.

But that first impression is misleading. GoвҖҷs simplicity is not accidental; it is engineered. And the deeper you go, the more you realize that what appears to be a minimal surface is actually a very deliberate design that forces you to understand what your code is doing. The simplicity is a doorway into depth.

People often misunderstand GoвҖҷs philosophy. They assume Go is вҖңsimple because itвҖҷs for beginners.вҖқ But Go was never made for beginners. It was built for teams at Google managing millions of lines of code, for systems that needed concurrency by default, for a world where codebases outlive their original authors. The language was designed so that the next person reading your code has an easy job вҖ” not because the author was brilliant, but because the language prevents unnecessary cleverness.

This is the core tension inside Go: it looks easy, but the moment you start asking the language behaves the way it does, you discover a surprisingly sophisticated ecosystem hidden under the surface.

Take memory, for example. Most beginners learn just enough to survive: variables go somewhere, the garbage collector cleans things up, pointers exist but you donвҖҷt need to think about them too deeply. But the moment you start digging вҖ” the stack, the heap, escape analysis, inline optimizations, tiny decisions the compiler makes on your behalf вҖ” the whole mental model of Go changes. The language that looked вҖңtoo simpleвҖқ suddenly reveals an internal machinery that is elegant, precise, and incredibly optimized.

Or take concurrency. A lot of beginners learn goroutines by launching a few lightweight threads and then synchronizing them using channels. They donвҖҷt yet see the invisible scheduler underneath: the M/N model, the run queues, the work stealing, the way goroutines migrate between threads, the tiny atomic operations happening at a micro-scale. All of that comes later, and it transforms how you write concurrent code forever.

And yet Go never forces this complexity on you. You can write code for months without knowing what escape analysis is. But if you want to grow beyond the basics, if you want to understand why certain patterns are fast, predictable, or idiomatic вҖ” you eventually confront the depth under the simplicity.

ThatвҖҷs what this series is about.

It's not a вҖңGo tutorial.вҖқ There are thousands of those. ItвҖҷs not another вҖңGo basics for beginnersвҖқ walkthrough вҖ” because those stop right when the interesting part begins. This series is for people who are new to Go but curious enough to look underneath the surface. Curious enough to care the compiler behaves the way it does. Curious enough to learn what actually happens when a goroutine sleeps, wakes, or blocks. Curious enough to understand the philosophy behind the design decisions.

The goal is not to overwhelm you. ItвҖҷs to give you the mental tools that most developers only acquire after years of experience. Tools that let you read Go code and understand its execution, not just its syntax. Tools that help you write programs that behave predictably under load. Tools that show you why Go encourages certain patterns and discourages others.

The simplicity of Go is an invitation. It removes noise so you can focus on ideas. But the ideas themselves run deep.

In the next chapters, weвҖҷll peel back this surface layer and explore the parts of Go that arenвҖҷt visible at first glance: how memory is allocated, moved, and freed; how concurrency is scheduled; why channels behave the way they do; how the compiler reasons about your code; why error handling looks the way it does; and what makes Go both accessible for beginners and powerful enough for large-scale systems.

If you're new to Go but want to understand it properly вҖ” not just use it, but feel how it works вҖ” youвҖҷre in the right place. The journey starts here, with a simple idea: Go isnвҖҷt small. ItвҖҷs focused. And once you begin exploring its hidden machinery, the language opens up in ways most beginners never get to see.

items.title: Mensageria com RabbitMQ e Golang - Conceitos e Primeiro Contato
items.author: Thiago JosГ© de Andrade Alvoravel
items.content:
A comunicaГ§ГЈo entre sistemas mudou bastante nos Гәltimos anos. Antes, era comum que uma aplicaГ§ГЈo chamasse outra diretamente e esperasse sua resposta. Isso funcionava, mas sГі atГ© certo ponto. Quanto maior o sistema, maior o acoplamento e menor a tolerГўncia a falhas. Hoje, quando falamos em microsserviГ§os, escalabilidade e resiliГӘncia, surge uma alternativa que atende diversos cenГЎrios: a mensageria.

A ideia central Г© simples. Em vez de depender de uma resposta imediata, o serviГ§o envia uma mensagem para uma fila e segue sua vida. Outro serviГ§o, quando puder, lГӘ essa mensagem e faz o trabalho necessГЎrio. Isso torna a aplicaГ§ГЈo mais leve, mais modular e mais preparada para lidar com perГӯodos de carga alta.

Neste artigo, vamos entender o bГЎsico sobre mensageria, aprender como usar o RabbitMQ para organizar o fluxo de mensagens e criar exemplos prГЎticos em Go.

O que Г© Mensageria e por que ela Г© tГЈo Гәtil

Mensageria Г© o padrГЈo de comunicaГ§ГЈo onde sistemas conversam atravГ©s do envio e consumo de mensagens, e nГЈo por chamadas diretas. Isso resolve alguns problemas comuns em arquiteturas tradicionais.

Imagine um serviГ§o de API que precisa realizar diversas etapas a partir de uma Гәnica requisiГ§ГЈo, sendo que uma delas (ou mais) pode sofrer com lentidГЈo devido a conexГЈo instГЎvel, grande volume de dados ou processamento mais pesado. Se for feito de forma sГӯncrona, basta um desses passos ficar lento para o serviГ§o todo travar, ou atГ© mesmo ocasionar em erros.

A mensageria permite deslocar essas tarefas para outro componente. Assim, a API recebe a requisiГ§ГЈo, separa o que precisa ser feito naquele momento (processamento sГӯncrono) e o que nГЈo precisa ser concluГӯdo antes do retorno da API (processamento assГӯncrono).

Por exemplo, se houver a necessidade dessa requisiГ§ГЈo enviar um email ao final, a API pode retornar uma mensagem informando que o email serГЎ enviado, e designar o disparo desse email a um serviГ§o de mensageria e que serГЎ feito de forma independente.

Uma cenГЎrio comum desse exemplo seria o enviar o e-mail de boas-vindas apГіs um cadastro. Ao invГ©s de ser feito de forma assГӯncrona na requisiГ§ГЈo, pode ser criada uma estrutura similar a esta:

Mensagem:
{
  "usuario_id": 123,
  "acao": "enviar_email_boas_vindas"
}

Isso vai para uma fila de mensagens. Depois, um outro serviГ§o lГӘ essa fila e manda o e-mail.

O que Г© RabbitMQ e como ele funciona

RabbitMQ Г© um message broker. Ele recebe mensagens, organiza essas mensagens em filas e garante que consumidores possam acessГЎ-las com seguranГ§a. RabbitMQ implementa o protocolo AMQP, que define regras claras para envio, roteamento e entrega de mensagens.

O RabbitMQ se destaca pela simplicidade, estabilidade e pela facilidade de adaptaГ§ГЈo a diferentes cenГЎrios вҖ” desde pequenas aplicaГ§Гөes atГ© sistemas de alta disponibilidade. Dentre alternativas conhecidas estГЈo Kafka, Redis Streams e SQS.

Alguns elementos bГЎsicos e fundamentais do RabbitMQ sГЈo: вҖў : Quem envia a mensagem вҖў : Quem lГӘ e processa a mensagem вҖў : Recebe a mensagen e a redireciona вҖў : Onde a mensagem fica armazenada вҖў : AssociaГ§ГЈo, ou vГӯnculo, entre uma exchange e uma fila вҖў : Chave usada para auxiliar no roteamento

O Exchange Г© a entidade na qual o Produtor publica mensagens, que sГЈo entГЈo roteadas para um conjunto de Filas. O objetivo do Exchange Г© encaminhar todas as mensagens que passam por ele para uma ou mais Filas ou outros Exchanges. O tipo de Exchange e as propriedades do Binding sГЈo usados вҖӢвҖӢpara implementar a lГіgica de roteamento.

Uma Fila no RabbitMQ Г© uma coleГ§ГЈo ordenada de mensagens. As mensagens sГЈo enfileiradas (criadas pelo Produtor) e desenfileiradas (entregues aos Consumidores) de maneira FIFO ("primeiro a entrar, primeiro a sair").

Exemplo 1 - Conectando ao RabbitMQ com Go

Esse artigo nГЈo tem como foco ensinar Go, ou apresentar a linguagem para quem nГЈo a conhece. Ou seja, nГЈo irei detalhar a estrutura de um projeto em Go, arquivos de configuraГ§ГЈo, dependГӘncias ou similares. Parto do princГӯpio que se vocГӘ quiser replicar os cГіdigos mostrados aqui, saberГЎ como criar uma estrutura bГЎsica para executar o cГіdigo.

TambГ©m nГЈo irei entrar em detalhes de como instalar RabbitMQ e Go, afinal existem diferentes maneiras de como vocГӘ pode querer fazer (Linux, Mac, Windows, Docker, etc), para isso recomendo os links abaixo das documentaГ§Гөes oficiais. ApГіs instalar ambos, podemos dar continuidade:

Vamos comeГ§ar criando um projeto em Go. O comando abaixo irГЎ criar o(s) arquivo(s) necessГЎrio(s) dentro da pasta que vocГӘ o executar:

go mod init exemplo/mensageria

PrГіximo passo Г© criar um arquivo (fiz um chamado ) e colar o cГіdigo abaixo. Esse exemplo Г© algo simples, que apenas abre uma conexГЈo com o RabbitMQ e garante que estГЎ funcionando:

package main

import (
    "log"

    amqp "github.com/rabbitmq/amqp091-go"
)

func main() {
    conn, err := amqp.Dial("amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672/")
    if err != nil {
        log.Fatalf("Erro ao conectar: %s", err)
    }
    defer conn.Close()

    log.Println("ConexГЈo bem-sucedida com RabbitMQ!")
}

Como criamos o projeto agora, precisamos baixar as dependГӘncias e depois executar o cГіdigo. Para isso devemos executar os comandos abaixo dentro da pasta do projeto, que foi criada anteriormente:

Se o serviГ§o do RabbitMQ estiver rodando, teremos a mensagem de sucesso no log:

Certifique-se de utilizar os parГўmetros corretos para conectar ao RabbitMQ pela funГ§ГЈo amqp.Dial("amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672"), caso vocГӘ tenha mudado algum durante ou apГіs a instalaГ§ГЈo, onde:

  • amqp - Protocolo usado pelo RabbitMQ. AMQP Г© o default e mais moderno
  • guest:guest - UsuГЎrio e senha do RabbitMQ. Valores usados sГЈo os default
  • localhost - URL de hospedagem do serviГ§o RabbitMQ, nesse caso local
  • 5672 - Porta usada pelo serviГ§o RabbitMQ, tambГ©m Г© usado um valor default aqui

Exemplo 2 - ProduГ§ГЈo e Consumo de uma Mensagem

Agora vamos para um exemplo mais prГіximo da realidade: criar um produtor, que irГЎ criar uma mensagem e colocГЎ-la em uma fila, e um consumidor para ler essa mensagem e exibir uma mensagem no log da aplicaГ§ГЈo.

Para isso teremos 2 pastas diferentes, para simular um cenГЎrio onde existem 2 aplicaГ§Гөes distintas e que sГЈo executadas de forma independente. Podemos repetir o passo do exemplo anterior para criar a estrutura, alГ©m de aproveitar tambГ©m o cГіdigo usado para conexГЈo com o serviГ§o do RabbitMQ. Lembrando de rodar os comandos abaixo em pastas diferentes e preferencialmente, para facilitar a localizaГ§ГЈo, no mesmo nГӯvel de hierarquia de pastas:

go mod init exemplo/produtorgo mod init exemplo/consumidor

Novamente, seguindo os passos do exemplo anterior, dentro de cada projeto vamos criar um arquivo com o cГіdigo Go. Abaixo estГЎ o cГіdigo completo do produtor, o qual explicarei algumas partes na sequГӘncia:

package main

import (
    "context"
    "encoding/json"
    "log"
    "time"

    amqp "github.com/rabbitmq/amqp091-go"
)

func failOnError(err error, msg string) {
    if err != nil {
        log.Panicf("%s: %s", msg, err)
    }
}

type User struct {
    UserType int    `json:"user_type"`
    Id       int    `json:"id"`
    Name     string `json:"name"`
}

func main() {
    conn, err := amqp.Dial("amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672/")
    failOnError(err, "Failed to connect to RabbitMQ")
    defer conn.Close()

    ch, err := conn.Channel()
    failOnError(err, "Failed to open a channel")
    defer ch.Close()

    err = ch.ExchangeDeclare(
        "my_topic", // name
        "topic",    // type
        true,       // durable
        false,      // auto-deleted
        false,      // internal
        false,      // no-wait
        nil,        // arguments
    )
    failOnError(err, "Failed to declare an exchange")

    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
    defer cancel()

    user1 := User{
        UserType: 1,
        Id:       10,
        Name:     "Paulo",
    }
    user1Json, _ := json.Marshal(user1)

    user2 := User{
        UserType: 2,
        Id:       20,
        Name:     "Maria",
    }
    user2Json, _ := json.Marshal(user2)

    // Envio de json
    err = ch.PublishWithContext(ctx,
        "my_topic", // exchange
        "type_1",   // routing key
        false,      // mandatory
        false,      // immediate
        amqp.Publishing{
            ContentType:  "application/json",
            Body:         []byte(user1Json),
        })

    failOnError(err, "Failed to publish a message for User 1")
    log.Printf(" [x] Sent %s\n", user1Json)

    err = ch.PublishWithContext(ctx,
        "my_topic", // exchange
        "type_2",   // routing key
        false,      // mandatory
        false,      // immediate
        amqp.Publishing{
            ContentType:  "application/json",
            Body:         []byte(user2Json),
        })

    failOnError(err, "Failed to publish a message for User 2")
    log.Printf(" [x] Sent %s\n", user2Json)
}

O primeiro ponto Г© entender, de forma macro, o que esse cГіdigo faz. HГЎ uma struct chamada com 3 campos: , e . Nosso produtor irГЎ instanciar variГЎveis do tipo e, para cada variГЎvel, produzir uma mensagem no nosso serviГ§o do RabbitMQ. Ao longo do cГіdigo hГЎ tambГ©m algumas checagens para analisar se houveram erros em diferentes etapas (conectar com RabbitMQ, declarar uma Exchange, publicar uma mensagem, etc). Vamos ver alguns trechos referente Г  produГ§ГЈo da mensagem:

err = ch.ExchangeDeclare(
    "my_topic", // name
    "topic",    // type
    true,       // durable
    false,      // auto-deleted
    false,      // internal
    false,      // no-wait
    nil,        // arguments
)

A funГ§ГЈo irГЎ criar uma Exchange com o nome . Caso jГЎ exista uma com esse nome, Г© verificado se ambas possuem os mesmos parГўmetros e, se sim, ela Г© utilizada ao invГ©s de criar uma duplicata.

O tipo da Exchange foi declarado como . Existem diferentes tipos, cada um com sua distinГ§ГЈo e contextos mais adequados para uso. O tipo permite que possamos fazer o vГӯnculo dessa mensagem com uma ao produzir uma mensagem. Isso Г© Гәtil, pois podemos produzir mensagens dentro de uma mesma Fila e Exchange, mas separГЎ-las por diferentes e as ler por diferentes consumidores (ou pelo mesmo consumidor em situaГ§Гөes diferentes).

Por exemplo, estamos produzindo mensagens com 2 usuГЎrios, cada um com o diferente. Podemos querer/precisar aplicar lГіgicas diferentes para cada tipo. Usar uma diferente para cada permite que a implementaГ§ГЈo fique mais desacoplada no consumidor, usando a mesma Exchange e Fila jГЎ que estamos criando um usuГЎrio em ambos os casos. Veremos isso na prГЎtica mais a frente.

true e false permitem que a Exchange continue existindo mesmo apГіs o servidor do RabbitMQ for reiniciado e tambГ©m quando nГЈo houverem mais Filas vinculadas a ela. false nГЈo permite que a Exchange aceite mensagens publicadas para ela. Mais Гәtil para contextos onde a Exchange serГЎ para uso interno ou privado, nГЈo ficando pГәblica/visГӯvel para usuГЎrios do serviГ§o. true Permite criar a Exchange sem esperar a confirmaГ§ГЈo do servidor se a mesma foi criada com sucesso (no exemplo deixamos como false, pois queremos o retorno do servidor). permite o envio de parГўmetros adicionais e opcionais, geralmente usado quando o tipo da Exchange demanda essas informaГ§Гөes.

err = ch.PublishWithContext(ctx,
        "my_topic", // exchange
        "type_1",   // routing key
        false,      // mandatory
        false,      // immediate
        amqp.Publishing{
            ContentType:  "application/json",
            Body:         []byte(user1Json),
        })

A funГ§ГЈo vai ser responsГЎvel por publicar a mensagem no servidor do RabbitMQ. O parГўmetro informa o nome da Exchange, que criamos anteriormente. Г© a chave que vai vincular a mensagem publicada com a Exchange informada. Se o parГўmetro for true, a mensagem nГЈo serГЎ entregue ao RabbitMQ caso nГЈo haja uma Fila para consumГӯ-la. O campo tem o mesmo propГіsito do , mas funciona para caso nГЈo haja um consumidor associado a uma Fila que corresponda a essa mensagem. informa a estrutura de dados que vai ser usada no conteГәdo da mensagem, que no nosso caso Г© uma instГўncia da struct convertida para json. sГЈo os dados em si que serГЈo enviados.

Foram publicadas 2 mensagens na mesma Exchange, cada uma diferente: e . No cГіdigo do consumidor veremos que mesmo na mesma Exchange e Fila, podemos consumir apenas uma ou ambas. TambГ©m sГЈo exibidas mensagens no log apГіs a publicaГ§ГЈo de cada mensagem:

Vamos agora ao cГіdigo do consumidor:

package main

import (
    "encoding/json"
    "log"

    amqp "github.com/rabbitmq/amqp091-go"
)

func failOnError(err error, msg string) {
    if err != nil {
        log.Panicf("%s: %s", msg, err)
    }
}

type User struct {
    UserType int    `json:"user_type"`
    Id       int    `json:"id"`
    Name     string `json:"name"`
}

func main() {
    conn, err := amqp.Dial("amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672/")
    failOnError(err, "Failed to connect to RabbitMQ")
    defer conn.Close()

    ch, err := conn.Channel()
    failOnError(err, "Failed to open a channel")
    defer ch.Close()

    err = ch.ExchangeDeclare(
        "my_topic", // name
        "topic",    // type
        true,       // durable
        false,      // auto-deleted
        false,      // internal
        false,      // no-wait
        nil,        // arguments
    )
    failOnError(err, "Failed to declare an exchange")

    q, err := ch.QueueDeclare(
        "my_topic", // name
        true,       // durable
        false,      // auto-deleted
        false,      // exclusive
        false,      // no-wait
        nil,        // arguments
    )
    failOnError(err, "Failed to declare a queue")

    log.Printf(" [*] Queue name: %s", q.Name)

    err = ch.QueueBind(
        q.Name,     // queue name
        "type_1",   // routing key
        "my_topic", // exchange
        false,      // no-wait
        nil,        // arguments
    )
    failOnError(err, "Failed to bind a queue")

    log.Printf(" [*] Waiting for messages. To exit press CTRL+C")

    msgs, err := ch.Consume(
        q.Name, // queue
        "",     // consumer
        true,   // auto-ack
        false,  // exclusive
        false,  // no-local
        false,  // no-wait
        nil,    // args
    )
    failOnError(err, "Failed to register a consumer")

    log.Printf(" [*] Reading message")

    for d := range msgs {
        var user User
        json.Unmarshal(d.Body, &user)
        log.Printf("Received a message in queue %s: %v", q.Name, user)
    }

    ch.Close()
}

O inГӯcio do cГіdigo Г© similar ao do produtor: definiГ§ГЈo da mesma struct usada, estabelecimento de conexГЈo com o serviГ§o do RabbitMQ e declaraГ§ГЈo da Exchange. Em seguida temos a declaraГ§ГЈo da Fila:

q, err := ch.QueueDeclare(
    "my_topic", // name
    true,       // durable
    false,      // auto-deleted
    false,      // exclusive
    false,      // no-wait
    nil,        // arguments
)

Nossa Fila foi criada com o mesmo que a Exchange, e tambГ©m com os mesmos valores para os parГўmetros , , e jГЎ que servem para os mesmos propГіsitos. O campo , se for true, serve para informar se a Fila serГЎ acessГӯvel apenas pela conexГЈo que a criou e irГЎ deletar a Fila quando essa mesma conexГЈo for fechada, que fazemos ao final do cГіdigo com a funГ§ГЈo .

Seguindo para o prГіximo trecho, temos a funГ§ГЈo . Ela Г© responsГЎvel por vincular a Fila que criamos com a Exchange declarada anteriormente. Essa mesma Exchange foi criada no cГіdigo do produtor (caso o mesmo seja executado primeiro) e, como foi explicado na parte do cГіdigo do produtor, por ela ainda existir no serviГ§o do RabbitMQ serГЎ reutilizada aqui:

err = ch.QueueBind(
    q.Name,     // queue name
    "type_1",   // routing key
    "my_topic", // exchange
    false,      // no-wait
    nil,        // arguments
)

Os parГўmetros e tГӘm a mesma lГіgica jГЎ vista anteriormente. e correspondem aos nomes da Fila e Exchange, respectivamente. O parГўmetro indica qual chave, que foi usada ao produzir a mensagem para a Exchange, iremos vincular a nossa Fila.

Voltando ao cГіdigo do produtor, vemos que 2 mensagens foram criadas: uma com a e outra com . Ou seja, aqui nossa Fila estГЎ vinculada apenas com a e iremos consumir apenas as mensagens da Exchange que usaram essa . A mensagem que produzimos com a ficarГЎ na Exchange sem nenhuma Fila para consumГӯ-la nesse momento.

Por fim temos o Гәltimo trecho para analisar. A funГ§ГЈo , que Г© responsГЎvel por consumir as mensagens da Fila que criamos e vinculamos com a Exchange:

msgs, err := ch.Consume(
    q.Name, // queue
    "",     // consumer
    true,   // auto-ack
    false,  // exclusive
    false,  // no-local
    false,  // no-wait
    nil,    // args
)

O parГўmetro informa o nome da Fila. O serve para atribuir uma tag (ou identificador) ao consumidor da Fila, que estamos criando. quando true serve para informar ao servidor que somente este consumidor irГЎ acessar esta Fila. Г© um parГўmetro existente no protocolo AMQP, porГ©m o RabbitMQ nГЈo dГЎ suporte ao mesmo, portanto o valor Г© ignorado.

true serve para informar ao serviГ§o do RabbitMQ, de modo automГЎtico, que a mensagem foi entregue ao consumidor. Isso faz com que ela seja removida da Fila de mensagem no momento que o consumidor a lГӘ. A depender do contexto, isso pode gerar problemas. Por exemplo, o serviГ§o de envio de email ficou fora do ar no meio do processamento. Nesse caso a mensagem serГЎ removida da Fila sem que possamos enviar o email. Uma soluГ§ГЈo seria deixar parГўmetro como false e, apГіs processar com sucesso tudo que for necessГЎrio, informar manualmente ao servidor que a mensagem foi entregue e ela jГЎ pode ser apagada da Fila.

ApГіs declarar o consumidor, temos um loop para exibir no log os dados do usuГЎrio que foram publicados na Fila. Ao executar esse cГіdigo devemos ter uma saГӯda similar a esta:

Note que foi exibido apenas os dados do usuГЎrio Paulo. Isso porque a outra mensagem, com os dados da usuГЎria Maria, foi publicada com a e a Fila que criamos foi vinculada a . Se quisermos consumir essa outra mensagem, poderГӯamos ter um outro consumidor com outra Fila vinculada a ela, ou entГЈo criar um outro com a mesma Fila mudando o . Assim o nosso consumidor conseguiria ler mensagens de ambos os .

Nesse texto vimos uma introduГ§ГЈo Г  mensageria, entendemos como funciona o RabbitMQ e como integrar ele com Go. Apesar de nГЈo ser possГӯvel abordar todas as combinaГ§Гөes e funcionalidades que ambos oferecem, pudemos constatar que essa combinaГ§ГЈo atende bem a diversos contextos e diferentes cenГЎrios.

items.title: Dependency Injection in Go: Patterns & Best Practices
items.author: Rost
items.content:
Dependency injection (DI) is a fundamental design pattern that promotes clean, testable, and maintainable code in Go applications.

What is Dependency Injection?

Dependency injection is a design pattern where components receive their dependencies from external sources rather than creating them internally. This approach decouples components, making your code more modular, testable, and maintainable.

In Go, dependency injection is particularly powerful because of the language's interface-based design philosophy. Go's implicit interface satisfaction means you can easily swap implementations without modifying existing code.

Why Use Dependency Injection in Go?

: By injecting dependencies, you can easily replace real implementations with mocks or test doubles. This allows you to write unit tests that are fast, isolated, and don't require external services like databases or APIs.

: Dependencies become explicit in your code. When you look at a constructor function, you immediately see what a component requires. This makes the codebase easier to understand and modify.

: Components depend on abstractions (interfaces) rather than concrete implementations. This means you can change implementations without affecting dependent code.

: You can configure different implementations for different environments (development, testing, production) without changing your business logic.

Constructor Injection: The Go Way

The most common and idiomatic way to implement dependency injection in Go is through constructor functions. These are typically named and accept dependencies as parameters.

Here's a simple example demonstrating constructor injection:

This pattern makes it clear that requires a . You cannot create a without providing a repository, which prevents runtime errors from missing dependencies.

When a component has multiple dependencies, simply add them as constructor parameters:

Interface Design for Dependency Injection

One of the key principles when implementing dependency injection is the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP): high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions.

In Go, this means defining small, focused interfaces that represent only what your component needs:

The smaller interface follows the Interface Segregation PrincipleвҖ”clients shouldn't depend on methods they don't use. This makes your code more flexible and easier to test.

Real-World Example: Database Abstraction

When working with databases in Go applications, you'll often need to abstract database operations. Here's how dependency injection helps:

This pattern is especially useful when implementing multi-tenant database patterns, where you might need to switch between different database implementations or connection strategies.

The Composition Root Pattern

The is where you assemble all your dependencies at the application's entry point (typically ). This centralizes dependency configuration and makes the dependency graph explicit.

This approach makes it clear how your application is structured and where dependencies come from. It's particularly valuable when building REST APIs in Go, where you need to coordinate multiple layers of dependencies.

Dependency Injection Frameworks

For larger applications with complex dependency graphs, managing dependencies manually can become cumbersome. Go has several DI frameworks that can help:

Google Wire (Compile-Time DI)

is a compile-time dependency injection tool that generates code. It's type-safe and has no runtime overhead.

go github.com/google/wire/cmd/wire@latest

Wire generates the dependency injection code at compile time, ensuring type safety and eliminating runtime reflection overhead.

is a runtime dependency injection framework that uses reflection. It's more flexible but has some runtime cost.

  • Your dependency graph is complex with many interdependent components
  • You have multiple implementations of the same interface that need to be selected based on configuration
  • You want automatic dependency resolution
  • You're building a large application where manual wiring becomes error-prone

Stick with manual DI when:

  • Your application is small to medium-sized
  • The dependency graph is simple and easy to follow
  • You want to keep dependencies minimal and explicit
  • You prefer explicit code over generated code

Testing with Dependency Injection

One of the primary benefits of dependency injection is improved testability. Here's how DI makes testing easier:

This test runs quickly, doesn't require a database, and tests your business logic in isolation. When working with ORM libraries in Go, you can inject mock repositories to test service logic without database setup.

Common Patterns and Best Practices

1. Use Interface Segregation

Keep interfaces small and focused on what the client actually needs:

2. Return Errors from Constructors

Constructors should validate dependencies and return errors if initialization fails:

3. Use Context for Request-Scoped Dependencies

For dependencies that are request-specific (like database transactions), pass them via context:

Don't inject dependencies that are truly internal implementation details. If a component creates and manages its own helper objects, that's fine:

Use comments to document why dependencies are needed and any constraints:

When NOT to Use Dependency Injection

Dependency injection is a powerful tool, but it's not always necessary:

  • Simple value objects or data structures
  • Internal helper functions or utilities
  • One-off scripts or small utilities
  • When direct instantiation is clearer and simpler

Example of when NOT to use DI:

Integration with Go Ecosystem

This allows you to swap PDF generation implementations or use mocks during testing.

Dependency injection is a cornerstone of writing maintainable, testable Go code. By following the patterns outlined in this articleвҖ”constructor injection, interface-based design, and the composition root patternвҖ”you'll create applications that are easier to understand, test, and modify.

Start with manual constructor injection for small to medium applications, and consider frameworks like Wire or Dig as your dependency graph grows. Remember that the goal is clarity and testability, not complexity for its own sake.

For more Go development resources, check out our Go Cheatsheet for quick reference on Go syntax and common patterns.

items.title: A Minimal Go Toolkit for Cleaning, Validating, and Querying CSV/TSV/Excel/Parquet Files
items.author: nchika
items.content:
In many software systems, not all data lives inside a database. Sometimes itвҖҷs stored in structured files such as CSVs, TSVs, or spreadsheets. And in practice, these files are not always clean. Data may be entered manually by someone who isnвҖҷt familiar with the format, values may be missing, and rows may contain inconsistencies that break downstream processing.

When the file is large, trying to вҖңfix it in ExcelвҖқ often leads to crashes вҖ” or simply makes it difficult to identify where the invalid records are.

I faced these issues repeatedly in real projects, and eventually decided to build a set of libraries to address them. Since IвҖҷm a Go developer, adopting the Python ETL ecosystem was never my first choice.

Instead, I created three small Go libraries that focus on preprocessing, validation, and lightweight analysis of structured file data:

  • fileprep вҖ” preprocessing + field-level validation using struct tags
  • fileframe вҖ” a tiny, immutable DataFrame for filtering and inspection
  • filesql вҖ” run SQL directly on CSV/TSV/LTSV/Excel/Parquet via embedded SQLite

These libraries are independent from one another. What connects them is the shared use of the interface. By passing data as a familiar , you donвҖҷt have to think about any custom data structures inside the libraries вҖ” you can work with the standard Go primitives you already know.

Now, let me explain the characteristics of each library.

  • Preprocessing: trim, replace, Unicode normalization, type coercion, etc.
  • Clear error reporting: identifies which row and which column caused the failure
  • Supports composite validation (cross-column rules)
  • Works with CSV/TSV/LTSV/Parquet/Excel вҖ” anything fed through 
Processed 2 rows, 2 valid
Name: , Email: 
Name: , Email:
  • Filtering, mapping, grouping
  • Ideal for вҖңone-time transformationsвҖқ on small/medium CSV/TSV datasets
Total rows: 5
Columns: product amount category]
Rows with amount  100: 2
Fruit: 370
Vegetable: 170

filesql is not вҖңSQL on filesвҖқ in the literal sense. Internally, it loads your data into a temporary SQLite database, giving you SQL capabilities without requiring you to manage the database yourself.

If you're a Go developer who doesnвҖҷt need the full Apache or Python ETL ecosystems, these three lightweight libraries may fit your workflow well.

The user base is still small, so there may be undiscovered bugs вҖ” feedback and issue reports are very welcome.

items.title: Bifrost: A Drop-in LLM Proxy, 80x Faster Than LiteLLM & Fully OSS
items.author: Debby McKinney
items.content:
When youвҖҷre building with LLMs, day-to-day tasks like writing, brainstorming, and quick automation feel almost effortless. But as soon as you try to construct a robust, production-grade pipeline, the real challenges emerge. One of the first hurdles is interface fragmentation: every provider exposes a different API, with its own rate limits, input quirks, and error formats. Switching models can mean rewriting large parts of your stack just to keep things running.

And yes, thatвҖҷs a real problem. But itвҖҷs also the obvious one.

LLM Gateways are great - until they become your bottleneck. Most LLM gateways do a good job at first: they unify APIs, hide output quirks, and let you swap providers without rewriting your code. It feels like future-proofing, until you scale. Once you're handling thousands of RPS, juggling live fallbacks, key rotation, latency SLAs, and token-level accounting, those clean abstractions start to buckle. What helped you move fast in dev becomes the bottleneck in prod.

ThatвҖҷs why we built Bifrost - not just another LLM proxy, but the fastest, most scalable LLM gateway out there, engineered specifically for high-throughput, production-grade AI systems.

вҡЎпёҸ Blazing fast: Built in Go, Bifrost introduces <15Вөs* internal overhead per request at 5000 RPS. рҹ“Ҡ First-class observability: Native Prometheus metrics built-in - no wrappers, no sidecars, just drop it in and scrape.

рҹ”Ң Flexible transport: Supports HTTP and gRPC (planned) out of the box, so you donвҖҷt have to contort your infra to fit the tool. Bifrost bends to your system, not the other way around.

Checkout the official bifrost github repository -

To give you an idea, we ran some benchmark tests at 500 RPS to compare performance of Bifrost and LiteLLM. Here are the results:

Beyond this scale, LiteLLM starts failing; resulting in more than 4 minutes latency on an average.

Both Bifrost and LiteLLM were benchmarked on a single instance for this comparison.

Throughput Comparison between Bifrost and LiteLLM (higher is better) ~9.5x faster, ~54x lower P99 latency, and uses 68% less memory than LiteLLM вҖ” on t3.medium instance (2 vCPUs) with tier 5 OpenAI Key.

Latency Comparison between Bifrost and LiteLLM (lower is better) We also ran a like-for-like benchmark based on the LiteLLM proxyвҖҷs own benchmarking setup - same load, same mocking behaviour, same hardware profile. Why? To get a clean, apples-to-apples comparison of real-world overheads from the gateway layer itself.

Below are the results comparing Bifrost and LiteLLM, both running on a single instance:

Note: Latency Overhead = Median latency - mocked OpenAI call latency (60ms). This includes request/response parsing and all middleware logic. If youвҖҷre building real LLM-powered products, Bifrost is the gateway designed to scale with you. No duct tape. No edge case rewrites. Just raw performance and production-ready control. In simple terms it's an abstraction layer that connects your application to multiple LLM providers with reliability, flexibility, and scale in mind.

How Bifrost is designed differently, and faster?

Single lightweight binary<15Вөs overhead, 5,000+ RPS
No callback hell, clean data patternsWrite custom plugins in minutes
Drop into any architecture
Works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, etc.
Built-in metrics, no setupProduction-ready observability

Instead of tying your logic to individual APIs, you talk to Bifrost. It handles the complexity behind the scenes: Key rotation, Provider fallbacks, Input/output normalization, Retry logic, observability, and versioed configuration. Whether you're building an AI-powered feature or handling millions of requests a day, Bifrost gives you a consistent, fast, and configurable foundation to build on, so you can focus on your product, not the plumbing.

What Bifrost Does (and DoesnвҖҷt) Do

Unified Gateway: Seamlessly connect to providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, Bedrock, and Cohere through a consistent, unified API. Fallback Mechanisms: Automatically falls back on failed requests to alternative providers, maintaining service continuity.

Key Management: Handles API keys across multiple accounts and providers, including key usage weightage across different providers and model-specific key restrictions.

Request/Response Normalization: Standardises inputs and outputs, allowing your application to remain agnostic to provider-specific formats.

Connection Pooling: Efficient sync pools reduce memory overhead and speed up execution (zero runtime memory allocation when configured right).

Full Configuration Control: Offers granular control over pool sizes, network retry settings, fallback providers, and network proxy configurations, ensuring optimal performance and adaptability. Bifrost provides sensible defaults but lets you tweak it for peak performance.

вқҢ Crash at scale: Bifrost is built to handle high-throughput traffic without buckling, even when things get extremely busy.

вқҢ Break your app with minor updates: We believe in versioned, predictable releases. No surprises, no silent regressions, and no вҖңwait, why did that stop working?вҖқ

вқҢ Control your business logic: Bifrost stays in its lane, handling LLM plumbing while you focus on building actual product value.

вқҢ Lock you into a single provider: You're free to switch or mix providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, Grok, or Bedrock without worrying about your LLM abstractions.

вқҢ Magically make you a better developer: But it will make your LLM stack cleaner, more scalable, and way less annoying to maintain.

How to Start Using Bifrost

Setting up Bifrost is very straightforward. There are two ways to use it: as an API server that your application calls, or directly as a Go package in your application.

  • Go 1.23 or higher (not needed if using Docker)
  • Access to at least one AI model provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)
  • API keys for the providers you wish to use

A. Using Bifrost as an HTTP Server

Create config.json: This file should contain your provider settings and API keys.

Setup your Environment: Add your environment variable to the session.

your_openai_api_key

Note: Make sure to add all the variables specified in your config.json file.

Start the Bifrost HTTP Server:You have two options to run the server, either using Go Binary or a Docker setup if go is not installed.

Install the transport package:

go github.com/maximhq/bifrost/transports/bifrost-http@latest

Run the server (make sure Go is present in the PATH):

bifrost-http  config.json  8080

curl  Dockerfile https://raw.githubusercontent.com/maximhq/bifrost/main/transports/Dockerfile

docker build ./config.json 8080  bifrost-transports

Run the Docker container:

docker run  8080:8080  OPENAI_API_KEY bifrost-transports

Note: Make sure to add all the variables specified in your config.json file.

Using the API: Once the server is running, you can send requests to the HTTP endpoints.

curl  POST http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions

рҹ’Ў For additional configurations in HTTP server setup, please read this.

Built in Prometheus/Grafana Support at /metrics endpoint.

B. Using Bifrost as a Go Package

Go Get Bifrost: Run the following command to install Bifrost as a golang package in your project.

go get github.com/maximhq/bifrost/core@latest

Implement Your Account Interface: You first need to create your account which follows Bifrost's account interface.

Bifrost uses these methods to get all the keys and configurations it needs to call the providers. You can check Additional Configurations for further customisations.

Initialize Bifrost: Set up the Bifrost instance by providing your account implementation.

Use Bifrost: Make your First LLM Call!

For more settings and configurations, check out the Documentation.

Bonus: You can use MaximвҖҷs pre-made plugin from github.com/maximhq/bifrost/plugins to add observability to Bifrost in just a single line!

go get github.com/maximhq/bifrost/plugins/maxim

Observability is only 1 step away!

Get your Maxim API Key and Log Repo ID from here.

WeвҖҷve stress-tested Bifrost under 5000 RPS on real AWS infrastructure to see how it performs under load. TL;DR: it holds up extremely well with single-digit microsecond latency overhead and full success rates, even under pretty lean configurations.

We ran Bifrost inside Docker containers with realistic memory and CPU limits, across two common EC2 instance types:

t3.medium (2 vCPUs, 4GB RAM)

Initial Pool Size: 10,000

t3.xlarge (4 vCPUs, 16GB RAM)

Initial Pool Size: 15,000

*Bifrost's overhead is measured at 59вҖҜВөs on t3.medium and 11вҖҜВөs on t3.xlarge, excluding the time taken for JSON marshalling and the HTTP call to the LLM, both of which are required in any custom implementation.

Note: On the t3.xlarge, we tested with significantly larger response payloads (~10 KB average vs ~1 KB on t3.medium). Even so, response parsing time dropped dramatically thanks to better CPU throughput and Bifrost's optimized memory reuse.

These arenвҖҷt synthetic hello-world tests. This is Bifrost running at 5K RPS, with full HTTP calls to upstreams, memory pooling enabled, and all core middlewares active.

Even the t3.medium (an entry-level instance) handled it without flinching.

On the xlarge, latency dropped across all internal steps.

Total overhead? Less than 15Вөs added per request on average.

All of this is configurable - you decide the pool sizes, retry logic, and buffer depths based on your needs. Want low memory? Dial the pools down. Want throughput? Turn it up. Either way, youвҖҷre not rewriting code, youвҖҷre just flipping knobs.

Curious? Run your own benchmarks. The Bifrost Benchmarking repo has everything you need to test it in your own environment.

Open Source and Built for the Community

Bifrost is fully open source, developed with transparency and extensibility at its core. It's licensed under Apache 2.0 and hosted on GitHub, where the codebase is actively maintained by contributions from the Maxim team at the moment. It's designed to be transparent, extensible, and community-driven from day one.

We believe infrastructure like this should be built in the open, shaped by real-world needs, and available for anyone to use, improve, or extend. Whether you're running Bifrost in production or experimenting on a side project, you're part of the community.

From clean abstractions to detailed documentation, everything in Bifrost is designed to be approachable. If somethingвҖҷs missing, confusing, or could be better, you're encouraged to help make it so.

WhatвҖҷs Next / Contribution Guide

WeвҖҷre just getting started. The core foundation of Bifrost is stable and production-ready, but the roadmap is full of exciting directions for more provider integrations and plugins. We welcome contributions of all kinds, whether it's bug fixes, features, documentation improvements, or new ideas. Feel free to open an issue, and once it's assigned, submit a Pull Request.

Here's how to get started (after picking up an issue):

  • Create your feature branch (git checkout -b feature/amazing-feature)
  • Commit your changes (git commit -m 'Add some amazing feature')
  • Push to the branch (git push origin feature/amazing-feature)
  • Open a Pull Request and describe your changes

Even if youвҖҷre not writing code, feedback, docs, use cases, and real-world stories are all valuable. Bifrost is better because of its community, and weвҖҷd love for you to be part of it.

items.title: A guide to managing 50+ local git repositories efficiently using Git-Scope, a TUI for polyrepo power users
items.author: Bharathkumar
items.content: How to Tame Your Multi-Repo Chaos with the Terminal

items.title: How to Tame Your Multi-Repo Chaos with the Terminal
items.author: Bharathkumar
items.content:
As a full-stack developer or SRE, you likely live in a world of microservices and polyrepos.

You have 10, 20, or maybe 50+ repositories cloned locally.

  • ...and that one side-project you forgot about.

The Problem: "Context Fatigue" рҹӣ‘

Every morning, the ritual is the same. navigate to each folder, checking if you forgot to push changes or if you're behind .

apps/frontend
git status
 ../../libs/ui-kit
git status

This manual context switching is a productivity killer. It pulls you out of your flow state before you even write a single line of code.

Existing GUIs (like GitHub Desktop or Sourcetree) are often too slow or bloated due to Electron. is amazing, but it's designed for focusing on repository at a time, not managing .

The Solution: A "God Mode" Dashboard for Git вҡЎпёҸ

I needed a tool that was:

  1. : Instant startup (written in Go).
  2. : I don't want to leave my CLI.
  3. : Shows me the state of my projects in one view.
  • : Automatically finds git repos in your folder.
  • : Instantly bubbles up repos with uncommitted changes so you never lose work.
  • : Type to jump to any project by name.

Who is this for? (ICP & Use Cases)

We built this for the вҖ”specifically:

1. The Microservices Backend Engineer рҹҸ—пёҸ

: You are working on a feature that spans 5 services (, , , etc.). You edited code in 3 of them but forgot which ones.: Open Git-Scope. The "Dirty" status bubbles all 3 modified repos to the top. You commit and push them in seconds, ensuring your PRs stay in sync.

2. The SRE / DevOps Engineer рҹӣ пёҸ

: You manage dozens of Terraform modules or Ansible roles. You need to check if your local copies are behind before running a plan.: One glance at the dashboard shows you exactly which infrastructure repos are out of date ( commits behind). You can pull updates without -ing into each directory.

3. The Open Source Maintainer рҹҗҷ

: You maintain 10+ libraries. You have random stashes and unpushed experimental branches scattered everywhere.: Use the () to instantly jump between libraries to review PRs locally. Use the "Clean" filter to find "abandoned" experiments you forgot to delete or push.

If you are on or , you can install it via Homebrew:

brew tap Bharath-code/tap
brew git-scope

Or install directly with :

go github.com/Bharath-code/git-scope/cmd/git-scope@latest

I wanted a tool that respects the "Unix Philosophy": do one thing and do it well.

doesn't try to replace or . It complements them. It's the high-level radar functionality that was missing from my workflow. It helps me and manage my local git universe without the friction.

Check it out on GitHub and let me know what you think!

items.title: Building DNS Resolution and Domain Services with Go: A Practical Guide
items.author: Jones Charles
items.content:
The internet is like a massive city where every website, service, or device has a unique address. The is the phonebook that translates human-friendly names like into IP addresses like . Without DNS, finding anything online would be a nightmareвҖ”like navigating a city without street signs.

Why use for DNS-related tasks? GoвҖҷs clean syntax, lightweight concurrency (hello, Goroutines!), and robust package make it a powerhouse for network applications. Plus, libraries like let you build custom DNS solutions, and GoвҖҷs single-binary deployment is a dream for production.

: This guide is for developers with some Go experience (1-2 years) who want to master DNS resolution and domain services. WeвҖҷll cover DNS basics, practical Go implementations, real-world use cases, and performance tips to help you build fast, reliable DNS systems.

DNS : The Basics You Need

DNS is the internetвҖҷs address resolver, turning domain names into IPs. HereвҖҷs how it works in a nutshell:

  1. : Checks if the IP is already cached.
  2. : Points to Top-Level Domain (TLD) servers (e.g., ).
  3. : Directs to the domainвҖҷs authoritative servers.
  4. : Return the IP or other records.
  • : Maps a domain to an IPv4 address (e.g., example.com вҶ’ 93.184.216.34).
  • : Same, but for IPv6.
  • : Aliases one domain to another (e.g., www.example.com вҶ’ example.com).
  • : Points to mail servers.
  • : Locates services (e.g., _http._tcp.example.com вҶ’ server:8080).

DNS isnвҖҷt just about IPsвҖ”it powers caching, load balancing, and service discovery in modern apps.

  • : and friends make simple queries a breeze.
  • : Goroutines handle thousands of queries efficiently.
  • : for custom DNS clients and servers.
  • : Cross-platform, single-binary apps simplify life.
  • Internal DNS for company networks.
  • Low-latency resolution for CDNs.
  • Service discovery in microservices.

LetвҖҷs dive into code and see Go in action!

Segment 2: Core DNS Resolution Techniques

Hands-On: DNS Resolution with Go

LetвҖҷs explore how to resolve DNS queries using GoвҖҷs standard library and build more advanced solutions with .

Simple DNS Queries with

The package is your go-to for quick DNS lookups. HereвҖҷs how to fetch A records for a domain:

  • : Enforces a 5-second timeout to prevent hangs.
  • : Queries A and AAAA records (UDP by default, TCP if needed).
  • : Great for quick scripts or prototypes.

: Always use for timeouts to avoid stuck queries.

Custom DNS Queries with

Need more control, like querying specific servers or record types? The library is your friend. HereвҖҷs how to query A and AAAA records:

  • : Specify upstream servers (e.g., GoogleвҖҷs ).
  • : Supports any record type (A, AAAA, MX, etc.).
  • : Add c.Timeout = 3 * time.Second to avoid hangs, and check to retry with TCP if the response is cut off.

Concurrent Queries with Goroutines

GoвҖҷs Goroutines make concurrent DNS queries a breeze. HereвҖҷs how to resolve multiple domains in parallel:

  • : Each query runs in its own Goroutine, speeding things up.
  • : Channels ensure thread-safe result collection.
  • : Use golang.org/x/sync/semaphore to limit Goroutines for high-volume queries.

Segment 3: Building a DNS Server and Optimizations

Building Your Own DNS Server

LetвҖҷs level up by creating a DNS server with that handles A records and supports load balancing with SRV records.

Simple DNS Server for A Records

HereвҖҷs a basic DNS server that responds to A record queries:

  • : responds with A records from a static map (replace with a database for real-world use).
  • : Uses to avoid root privileges needed for .
  • : Use dig @localhost -p 8053 example.com.

Load Balancing with SRV Records

For microservices, SRV records enable service discovery and load balancing. HereвҖҷs a server that returns SRV records:

  • : Weights (60:40) distribute traffic between servers.
  • : Perfect for microservices with tools like Consul.
  • : Dynamically update records using a service registry.

To make your DNS service scream, try these optimizations:

  1. : Use for thread-safe in-memory caching with TTLs (see Section 3.4 of the original for a code example).
  2. : Reuse TCP/UDP connections to cut overhead.
  3. : Enable in to shrink responses.
  • Concurrency: 50 вҶ’ QPS: ~1200, Latency: ~4ms
  • With caching: Latency drops to <1ms, QPS doubles.

Segment 4: Real-World Applications and Next Steps

HereвҖҷs how Go-powered DNS shines in the wild:

  1. : A company used to resolve internal domains, cutting latency from 50ms to 5ms with caching.
  2. : Custom DNS clients with connection pooling hit 10ms latency and 90% cache hit rates.
  3. : SRV records with Consul enabled 20ms service discovery for dynamic load balancing.

Common Pitfalls and Fixes:

  • : Use dynamic timeouts (1s intranet, 3s internet) with retries.
  • : Implement dynamic TTLs or LRU caching.
  • : Switch to TCP when is true.

Next Steps: Go Further with DNS

  1. : Encrypt queries for privacy (see Section 6.3 of the original for a DoH client example).
  2. : KubernetesвҖҷ default DNS server, built in Go, with plugins for everything.
  3. : Use tools like or GoвҖҷs logger to track QPS and latency.
  4. : Limit response sizes to prevent amplification attacks.

GoвҖҷs simplicity, concurrency, and libraries make it a killer choice for DNS projects. Whether youвҖҷre resolving domains, building servers, or optimizing for speed, Go has you covered. Try these examples, tweak them, and share your DNS adventures in the comments!

items.title: Shhh.. Go's getting a 'secret mode'
